## 理解直觉

**乔纳森·科恩：**
让我们回到普通人群。你是否相信，拥有直觉感知能力——无论以何种形式呈现——是人类与生俱来的独特禀赋？

**安娜·尤西姆：**
当然。但有些人会更重视并依赖直觉，从中获得更大效用，因此会因其先天特质、社会及家庭环境的影响而更积极发展这种能力。

另一些人则极度理性。即便他们有直觉，直觉也需通过理性过滤显现，他们甚至不会使用“直觉”这个词。我们需要稍广义地定义一下直觉。

我认为直觉可分为三类：第一类直觉，诺贝尔奖得主丹尼尔·卡尼曼在《思考，快与慢》中描述的“快思考系统”——更像是一种本能。就像模式识别系统：你莫名知晓某事，仅仅是认出了某种模式。这就是第一类直觉。

**梅伊姆·比亚利克：**
比如前几天我在医生那里，她提到各种事项。当我做出“我不想那样做”的表情时，那是直觉吗？还是单纯的不情愿？

**NA YUSIM：** 是的。对你来说，看到那个表情，她做了一个表情，你立刻就明白了它的含义。你不需要费力去琢磨那个表情是什么意思。你凭直觉就知道了，这几乎是一种本能。

第二种直觉，是指你和某人同处一室时的那种感觉，有点像你刚才说的，**Mayim**。就是你虽然和某人在一起，但你知道了某些事，却不知道为什么知道，而这并不是因为他们做出了某个特定的面部表情。这就像你在解读各种迹象，但你甚至不太清楚自己是怎么知道的。就像你见到某人，就会对他有一种直觉。

这方面有个例子：有研究表明，人们能够感知到别人在盯着自己看。所以，即使有人在你背后盯着你，你也会感觉到。哦，你不知道为什么知道，但你就是知道。你就是能够察觉到。对吧？这就是第二种直觉。

第三种直觉，就像是相隔世界的双胞胎之间的心灵感应。或者，当一个母亲知道一百多公里外的孩子出了什么事，你就是感觉到不对劲。“宝贝，你还好吗？” 类似这样的情况。所以，这是三种不同类型的直觉，虽然从技术上讲用词相似，但其含义和现象却截然不同。

**MAYIM BIALIK：** Jonathan 经常有直觉。

## 发展你的直觉

**JONATHAN COHEN：** 我相信我们都有能力发展直觉。如果有人想要发展更多直觉，并且感觉自己可能有过第一种体验，但他们真正想获得的是第三种或第四种直觉——比如能感知房间另一端的人，或者他们想产生连接、但可能不在同一物理空间的人，或者他们想要答案。他们想知道：“我的人生应该怎么做？” 他们想要直觉性的指引，而且不确定 ChatGPT 能给出最佳答案，即便它被用于此目的。我们如何开始帮助一个人发展他们的直觉？

**ANNA YUSIM：** 这是个非常重要的问题。因为直觉常常确实能给你理性无法给出的答案。你可以列出利弊清单，但真正的答案远比你的利弊清单来得深刻。利弊清单帮助你权衡选项和可能性，但它不一定是“我应该嫁给这个人吗？”或“我应该从事什么样的工作？”这类问题的答案。人生最重要的问题，往往是关乎心灵与灵魂的问题，而不一定是利弊权衡的问题。

因此，我认为要帮助人们发展直觉，首先显然要帮助他们发展理性，针对任何问题列出利弊清单。但同时，也要请他们尽自己所能去弄清楚事情，然后请他们花一两天时间把它暂时放在一边，但要询问他们是否愿意接受指引，以及他们希望从何处获得指引——是从祖先那里，从神那里，等等。去请求那种指引，并敞开心扉接受指引会如何显现。

那种指引可能以有意义的巧合显现。可能以直觉洞察的形式显现。可能以开始用一种新的眼光看待事物的方式显现。可能以意想不到之人的建议显现。你可能看一部电影，从而对某事获得新的视角。所以我认为，这些是一些方法：通过你的利弊清单和理性，你尽自己最大的努力；然后，寻求帮助。你提出想要获得直觉洞察的意愿，然后敞开心扉去接收那个洞察。

**JONATHAN COHEN：** 嗯，我认为重要的是，当我们思考直觉时，并非告诉人们摒弃逻辑思维。它们是相辅相成的。我听过一种描述：逻辑思维和理性思维能够看到的范围，就像在夜晚开车。车灯照亮的大约是前方200米的范围。但直觉或许能看到更远得多的地方，因为在那无限广阔的觉知领域中，可能有一些尚未发生的事情可以被理解或知晓。

## 可见与不可见的现实

**JONATHAN COHEN：** 这又引出了一个想法：如果只有1%的信息可以通过我们的眼睛和耳朵感知，那么有多少信息是在我们五种感官之外可以获知的呢？

**ANNA YUSIM：** 正是如此。这就是为什么发展我们其他的认知方式如此重要。而且，你需要发展它吗？如果你不发展，情况会更糟吗？也许会，也许不会。因为在西方世界通行的科学世界里，可见可闻的便是全部。所以你未必需要除此之外的任何东西。

但也有一些杰出的科学家是深度灵性的，比如弗朗西斯·柯林斯等等，他们真正引入了灵性的视角。如果你能增强直觉，这通常能帮助你工作的各个方面，因为你可以通过理性思维之外的其他方式接收信息。

**JONATHAN COHEN：** 请告诉我们您研究过或您所了解的同事们研究过的内容。您提到了超视能力或使用通灵术的人。关于这些方式，您能告诉我们些什么？它们是如何与接入那无限的意识领域相关，以获得实际和有形益处的，无论是物质利益还是情感利益？

**ANNA YUSIM：** 是的。我的意思是，在我自己的临床实践中，我有过一些失去亲人的患者，他们通过灵媒与那些亲人连接。对我的患者来说，这种情况往往——在过去15到20年里，我大概有七八个这样的患者。这些经历从来都不是中立的。它们也从来不是负面的，通常是积极的，或者非常非常积极，尤其是对于那些怀疑者而言，因为我有时会遇到在失去亲人后陷入困境、无法前进的怀疑者。

而灵媒实际上是那个为他们稍微打开局面的人。他们有过非常不可思议的体验。我们也要清楚，并非所有灵媒都一样。并非所有灵媒都是真实的。但确实有一些极具天赋的灵媒存在，他们能够建立连接，能够为人们提供具有深度疗愈作用的信息。所以，如果你打算这么做，请务必通过你认识且信任的人来验证灵媒，确保找到非常非常优秀的人。

**JONATHAN COHEN：** 在不涉及具体细节的情况下，人们通过这些通灵交流获得的信息类型有哪些，能够提供那种疗愈作用？

**MAYIM BIALIK：** 我接着说几句。我在这里要扮演一个唱反调的角色。我去过很多通灵者那里。是的。我去见过灵媒。我跟通灵者交谈过。是的。有些可能更靠谱，有些可能不那么靠谱。我只是想说，他们通常都会确认自己是靠谱的，而这可以通过多种方式做到。

他们通常会告诉你一些理论上他们本不该知道的事情。

但先来泼点冷水吧，通常传达的信息都是：很抱歉伤害了你。你将会做出了不起的成就。我解除所有让你痛苦的联结。我从未有意那样做。那不是我的灵魂的本意，你会好起来的。

**安娜·尤西姆：** 当你拥有这些经历时，它们是否对你有任何帮助呢？

**梅伊姆·比亚利克：** 没有。我想我的问题是，如果我们试图从另一个世界获取信息，我们可以寻求怎样的具体信息？因为我懂这些。爱、和平、共存。我明白。但我认为人们想从个人角度知道：为什么我会生病？我该如何好转？怎样才能让生活不那么艰难？还有，为什么世界如此混乱？

## 重大问题与灵性指引

**安娜·尤西姆：** 这些都是非常、非常大的问题，对吧？而且我感觉……是的，确实如此。它们真的是哲学性的问题。我相信一个好的通灵者可以就此给你提供指引。但很多时候，这些问题也是留给我们自己去回答的，你知道吗？并且，归根结底，我们才是为自己的人生赋予意义、厘清为何事情发生或为何没有发生的人。

我们能否从通灵媒介、李·哈里斯等人那里获得一个更高的视角？当然可以。我们能否从一位真正优秀的高阶通灵者那里得到意见？绝对可以。我们是否也会遇到有人说一些对我们毫无意义的老套陈词滥调？当然，我们都有过那样的经历，正如你，梅伊姆，恰如其分地指出的那样。

但我常常觉得，我不确定这些是否一定是……你知道，这很有趣，因为我经常做一个关于灵性的演讲。我会谈到灵性中的陷阱或有毒的部分。其中之一就是对通灵者和媒介的沉迷。有些人会对通灵者和媒介上瘾，为了得到一点钱，你就让别人控制你的生活，以便让他们告诉你什么是真的，给你某种确定性。

而结果真的成真了吗？也许，有时候吧。可能成真，也可能不会。看情况，你知道的。而且外面也有一些异常出色的通灵者。我当然遇到过。我也遇到过相反的，我相信我们都遇到过，你知道，但问题是……我认为问题在于，我们难以完全掌控自己的生活，以及我们渴望交出控制权，而当某人似乎能窥探未来并给你确定性时，这种渴望是多么容易让人上瘾。

本质上，对通灵者的上瘾就是对控制的上瘾。所以，认识到这一点也非常重要。另外，关于媒介，有时发生的情况是：某人已经去世，而另一个人可能深陷悲伤无法前进。有时候，媒介通过让此人与逝者建立连接，并证明这种通灵的真实性，能让这个人知道：哇，这个人确实走了。但他们仍然以某种形式陪伴着我，我仍然可以和他们交流。

对有些人来说，这正是他们需要继续前进的动力。我当然见过这种情况。这不是我倡导或……但在发生自杀事件的情况下，这种方式也非常、非常有力。

## 对确定性的沉迷

**乔纳森·科恩：** 沉迷于确定性、试图获得确定性的想法。有些人追求直觉，因为他们只是想在答案中获得确定性。但讽刺的是，无论你的直觉多强，你永远无法获得确定性。你可能会被引领到下一个最佳解决方案，但它仍然很可能不会完全按照你想象、思考或期望的方式发展。世界总是有这种出人意料的因素，在变化和演进中，我们无法逃避。

**安娜·尤西姆：** 确实如此。确实如此。你知道吗，如果你想更深入地探讨，不同的通灵者会读取不同的东西。有些人能读取阿卡西记录。有些人能读取你本身。有些人能读取你的第三眼，或者读取你与天堂的连接。读取这两种不同的东西会给你完全不同的解读，你知道的。

所以你并不确切知道，当他们在读取时，当他们在读取你时，他们读取的是什么，以及他们从哪里获取信息。

**梅伊姆·比亚利克：** 我们和许多在宇宙中做各种有趣事情的人交谈过。但我从未像和你谈话这样，了解这么多关于人们听到、读到和看到的东西。人们可以读取你的第三眼？

**安娜·尤西姆：** 是的。

**乔纳森·科恩：** 请告诉我们读取顶轮和读取第三眼的区别。

## 读取第三眼与顶轮的区别

**安娜·尤西姆：** 当然可以。当人们读取你的第三眼时，他们本质上是在读取你自己的直觉。你感觉如何？所以当你问某人，那个男的对我怎么看？他们要读取的，并不是去那个男人那里，实际上他们会来你这里，读取你认为那个男人对你的看法，然后反馈给你。这与从顶轮读取不同。

如果他们也能从自己的顶轮读取，并从技术上说是从神性或他们的灵性导师那里下载信息。所以有很多不同的方式。真的有很多不同的方式。而且你可能也有不同的灵性导师，他们可能对某件事有不同的看法。所以你可能会接收到……你知道，来自不同方面的信息。这很有趣。

**乔纳森·科恩：** 读取你的第三眼就像身处媒体的回音室。你只是被强化了你已有的信念，然后你就会觉得，哦，天哪，那个通灵者真厉害。但他们所做的只是告诉你你的感受，而不是客观现实是什么。

**安娜·尤西姆：** 是的，但是……需要澄清的是，有时，有人能丝毫不差地反映出你的感受（而你并未告诉他们任何信息），这可能具有难以置信的力量。你可能甚至不知道自己有那种感觉。然后他们可能将其反馈给你。然后你就会觉得，哇，太神奇了。但那是非常不同的一种解读。

**乔纳森·科恩：** 但那并不代表它就是真的。

**梅伊姆·比亚利克：** 不止一个前男友指责过我，说我好像比他们自己更了解他们，但我仍然认为我是对的。

**乔纳森·科恩：** 也许你读取了他们的第三眼。

**梅伊姆·比亚利克：** 这正是我想说的。我们通常称之为霸道、万事通、控制欲强的女友。也许那是与宇宙深刻、直觉性地对齐。女朋友。

**安娜·尤西姆：** 我喜欢这个说法。那确实……是的，肯定更吸引人。

## 灵性导师与守护天使

**乔纳森·科恩：** 这触及了我们实际上与托马斯·坎贝尔讨论过的一些东西，即从何处获取信息的概念。你可以从更大的意识系统中获取信息。你可以从个体的意识或你自己的意识中获取信息，而这些信息会有所不同。我们以为，哦，如果我试图去获取直觉，那直觉会是客观的。

但同样的调谐，安放你的意识很重要，因为那信息会不同。我对你刚才提到的导师和守护天使很感兴趣。第一个问题是：每个人都有导师和守护天使吗？第二个问题是：为什么他们的视角不同？他们难道不是朝着某种同步的目标努力吗？

**安娜·尤西姆：** 我确实相信，大多数人都有某种天使或守护天使、大天使，在某种程度上保护着他们，不同的人程度不同，这取决于他们在世界上的目的、他们的所作所为以及他们的生活方式。还有我认为他们需要什么以及……而且我认为只是不同的导师可能会提供不同的信息。

**乔纳森·科恩：** 也许 t

这里有工作指南、爱情指南和健康指南。你不能向所有指南询问同样的问题。

**安娜·尤西姆：** 没错。它们是不同的。要知道，你可以想象每个人都有不同的天赋、能力、兴趣和魅力。从本质上讲，我们是被它们引导着走过人生的。很多东西我们并非后天培养，而是与生俱来。因此，有的指南帮助我们在世界上以一种方式存在，有的则帮助我们发展这些存在的方式。

**乔纳森·科恩：** 你和你的指南有联系吗？你是如何建立这种联系的？

**安娜·尤西姆：** 是的，我有。我经常会问它们问题，但当我直觉来临时，我不知道它来自谁。我不确定是来自我的指南，来自上帝，还是来自我自己。但我确切地知道，我何时获得一个直觉或洞见。

**乔纳森·科恩：** 克莱尔，你认知的主要模式是什么？

**安娜·尤西姆：** 是“认知”。当我“就是知道”时，我不知道我是如何知道的，某个想法就会直接进入我的脑海。我认为这是因为我的思维能力相对于其他能力过于发达，这是信息进入我脑海或身心的最容易的方式。

**乔纳森·科恩：** 嗯，我曾尝试建立联系。

**梅伊姆·比亚利克：** 与指南建立联系，但你知道的，比所有的都好。

## 与指南建立关系

**乔纳森·科恩：** 不，从来没有。我从没真正弄清楚过。在我们采访了一些能接触到指南的人之后，我心想，哦，我确实相信周围有指南，我相信这一点。我也曾请求指南给予指引，但我不相信那些信息来自某个特定的指南。我并没有感觉到身边有一个“议会”，或者它们扮演着不同的角色。

但我确实感觉我经常通过听觉获取信息。通常只是一种“知晓”或一种共情的感觉，我听到的声音是我自己的一种版本，而不是别人。我并不是听到一个英国口音在与我交流并告诉我发生了什么。你听到声音，乔纳森，不是多个声音。我听到的是我自己声音的一个版本。有人可以称之为他们自己的一个版本。也许另一些人称之为“高我”。

我很好奇，尤西姆博士，您如何理解“高我”？它和指南或天使一样吗？还是只是我们认知的另一部分？对您来说它是什么？

**安娜·尤西姆：**

我很喜欢你写的（或说的）“我们认知的另一部分”。我认为那就是它的本质。我们将自身所有的面向合为一体。你可以用弗洛伊德的理论来理解它，即本我、自我和超我，对吧？本是我们最原始、最基本的本能。超我是我们的道德感，而自我是这两者的交集或整合，高我也是一样。

它是我们自身的一部分，在任何特定情境下都已经知道什么对我们最有利。关键在于，即使以你当前的形式，你也能接入这部分，因为有很多不同的观念，但其中一些认为时间有点像一种幻觉。所以你可以向前穿越到那个对未来有所知晓的自己。你也可以回到过去，与那个自己对话。有很多关于“内在小孩”的工作，有与你内在更年轻部分的互动，还有部分心理学。所有这些方式都能让你触及自己所有不同的面向。

因此，高我就是那个已经知晓、已经经历过、消化了并且从你正在经历的事情中成长起来、拥有更进化意识的那个部分。

**乔纳森·科恩：**

而且很有可能，这个部分不受我们之前讨论的任何潜意识程序的影响。它摆脱了此生的某些业力残留。它可能是我们的一部分，一直伴随着我们。如果我们相信前世，它可能就是那个历经我们存在多个版本的部分。

**安娜·尤西姆：**

是的，它可能是超越性灵魂的一部分。那个持续不断、生生不息的超越性部分，是我们的太阳精神。那是永不疲倦的部分，是我们永远不会失去，并且持续成长和扩展的部分。

## 灵媒的力量

**乔纳森·科恩：**

谈到灵媒以及帮助人们以不同视角看待事物的话题。我们曾与“长岛灵媒”特蕾莎·卡普托交谈。在那次节目中，她分享了一种感觉，对我产生了前所未有的巨大影响。

我的家庭在我14岁时遭遇了一场重大悲剧，它标记了我们此后三十多年的生活。她所讨论的事情发生在非物质领域。对于没听过那个故事或节目的人来说，我的哥哥在一次车祸中受了重伤，几乎丧生。

她谈到的是，当他在生死之间做决定时，我父亲的父亲介入其中，帮助将他送回来，继续活在这个世界上。而我祖父扮演的角色，实际上是为了支持我的父亲。

尽管我曾考虑过这个情况对家庭的影响，但我主要是从我哥哥的角度来看待受伤意味着什么，也从我自己的角度，部分地从我父亲的角度。但我从未真正理解她当时提供的那个信息。

想象一下，思考某件事、经历了35年，突然之间，出现了一个我从未体验过的新角度。仅仅是这位不知信息从何而来的女士，分享了那个小小的洞见。这非常强大。

**安娜·尤西姆：**

哇，这听起来极其强大。而且，当你收到这个信息时，它是否让你觉得……你是否“知道”这确实是真实的？它是否契合你理解世界的框架，还是立即扩展了你自己的认知体系？

**乔纳森·科恩：**

我从未考虑过这一点，但它立即被整合了。我的认知体系没有抗拒这个信息，没有说“哦，我不知道，这感觉不对”。完全没有。就像我从未想过一样。当我接收到它时，它立即嵌入进来，并扩展了我对整个经历的理解，使其感觉更加完整和圆满。

**安娜·尤西姆：**

这听起来如此强大。我们谈论过“奇迹”的定义是感知的转变。有时，那种引导你以略有不同的方式看待事物、改变你对生活中发生的如此重要和创伤性事件的整个看法的信息，会改变你的整个生命。所以，这听起来像是一个奇迹般的事情。

## 个人的觉醒

**乔纳森·科恩：**

你能跟我们谈谈你的个人觉醒经历吗？是否有一段时间你完全是个唯物主义者，没有超感官能力、直觉的经验，也没有与你的指南建立关系？你是如何从以前的尤西姆博士变成今天的自己的？

**安娜·尤西姆：**

如果25年前有人告诉我，我会成为一个有灵性的人，我会笑出来。灵性那时是我最不关心的事。我完成了本科学习，主修生物学和哲学。读医学院、做住院医师？都没有灵性，因为那就不是我。我有太多事情要做：我为医学院学习，我有恋爱关系，我做着普通的事情。然后，在我精神科住院医师实习期接近尾声时，开始发生一些有趣的事情，让我开始思考，哇，也许世界的运作方式与我一直以来被教导的有所不同。

那时我开始对“灵魂”的概念产生兴趣，因为我遇到了一个我当时认为是灵魂伴侣的人。他最终并非我的灵魂伴侣，但这绝对让我对这个过程产生了兴趣。我记得有一次去犹太教堂，听了一个讲座……

走在回家路上时，我被一家冰淇淋店吸引进去，买了份冰淇淋。正吃着，一位通灵师带着一个小孩子走近我，通灵师说：“我是通灵师，有信息要传达给你。可以告诉你吗？”

**梅伊姆·比亚利克：**

不行。

**安娜·尤西姆：**

我说：“好啊，当然。”在纽约市听起来这挺寻常的。于是她坐下来，这位女士开始向我讲述我生活中种种深刻的真相——那些她绝无可能知道的事，甚至包括我交往过的那个男人的名字。这些信息就这样从她口中流淌出来。

在那之后，我满心困惑：这到底是怎么回事？因为直到那一刻，我所接受的教育都告诉我们每个人的意识是独立且分离的——我活在我的世界，你活在你的世界。而这位女士，我不知道她是读取了我的思维，还是接收了某种更高维度的信息，但她确实知道了她不可能知道的事情。那些事我甚至没写在日记里，也没发在Facebook上。她绝无可能知晓，但她就是知道。

**梅伊姆·比亚利克：**

你给她买冰淇淋了吗？

**安娜·尤西姆：**

没有。不过我后来和她合作了一段时间。那算是整个经历的起点。从那时起，我开始不断接收到各种信息。我曾梦见一个写着“揭示卡巴拉”的招牌。我母亲几年前研究过卡巴拉，还给我寄过相关书籍。我心想：也许这只是我书架上的某本书名吧。

几周后，我去和朋友吃晚饭的路上，竟看到了和梦中一模一样的招牌——“揭示卡巴拉”，那是纽约卡巴拉中心。于是我就走了进去，心想：好吧，这应验了我的梦。我报名参加了课程，卡巴拉中心开始为我提供一个新的框架，帮助我看清和理解我正在学习的这一切。这让我过去多年学习的科学框架与刚刚开始接触的这个新框架之间产生了区分。非常有趣。

**乔纳森·科恩：**

是否有某个瞬间，你的直觉突然打开了？你是如何从最初接收那次解读和那些征兆，开始被同步性事件引导——听起来是这样——逐步走向一种更整合的灵性生活的？如今你似乎每天都在整合灵性与直觉。

**安娜·尤西姆：**

是的，这个过程非常缓慢渐进。生活中不断发生这类事件，我逐步消化理解，试图弄清到底是怎么回事。我该如何理解这些？最终在七年内，我从私人执业中积累了足够多的病例——50个案例，写成了一本书，后来得以出版。我也逐渐将自己重新定位为“灵性精神科医生”，整合精神病学与灵性，后来还在耶鲁大学推动建立了心理健康与灵性中心。

所以这个过程缓慢而渐进，并且持续进行。有些人天生擅长接收直觉信息。我也有直觉力，信息会来到我这里，但我希望进一步拓展自己的能力，也一直在朝这个方向努力。

## 灵魂伴侣与业力模式

**乔纳森·科恩：**

你相信我们有灵魂伴侣吗？

**安娜·尤西姆：**

我相信，很可能存在前世，也可能存在一些与我们有过交集、对我们至关重要的灵魂。这其中当然可能有某些课题和业力模式。灵魂伴侣确实存在。但某人是你的灵魂伴侣，并不意味着你们的关系会完美无瑕、一路玫瑰。它意味着存在一种业力模式，并且这是一种需要我们去理解和解决的重要业力模式。

所以，虽然我喜欢灵魂伴侣这个非常浪漫化的概念，但我也不认为它应该阻碍人们在生活中获得爱、喜悦和满足感。

**乔纳森·科恩：**

人们应该如何理解业力模式？

**安娜·尤西姆：**

业力模式基于“课题”与“多世生命”的理念。其核心思想是：我们并非只活这一世，灵魂在来到此生之前已历经多世轮回；我们曾有的行为、积累的某些业力，需要在此生通过行动——有时通过痛苦与磨难，有时通过积极的选择——来清除。也有人认为业力可以完全消除，你可以释放所有业力——如果真是如此，那是个很美好的观念。

但我们谈论过“重复”、“强迫”和“灵魂修正”这些概念。那些此生一再出现、成为挑战或困境的事情，尽管我们尽力改变却依然发生，常令我们懊恼沮丧——这些往往就是我们所谓的业力模式。它们是用不同词语描述的类似事物，是我们此生必须面对并超越的课题。

## 普世的灵性真理

**梅伊姆·比亚利克：**

你研究过许多不同类型的灵性和宗教传统，作为你工作的基础。你发现有哪些是普世的？你在南美、印度、以色列乃至全世界都学习过。你认为哪些共同主题是科学或许正在试图追赶和解释，但这些观念本身已存在数千年？如果让你说出最核心的三条，你认为伟大的神秘主义灵性传统有哪些共同点？

**安娜·尤西姆：**

我认为，跨越宗教和灵性传统的三个普世真理是：
第一，爱的普遍性——它是这世上最重要的事物之一，甚至就是最重要的事。爱的定义多种多样，包括爱自己、爱他人、爱神。这是第一条。

第二，服务的重要性，即奉献自我的能力，通过各种方式和途径服务他人。这关乎你在这个世界的独特性——你能以一种无人能替代的方式提供服务，也关乎怀着奉献之心去接受，为了给予他人，并确保你在生活中创造这样一种流动：你始终在给予。

第三是使命，这也与给予有关，但更深刻。它是指明了你为何在此——一个人的人生目标，以及在此生为实现其全部潜能所需要做的工作。所以我认为，这三点是渗透于不同宗教和灵性传统中最主要的普世真理。

**梅伊姆·比亚利克：** 这真的很有趣，也很令人安慰。我就觉得你会提到爱，因为从许多关于裸盖菇素辅助治疗、甚至氯胺酮辅助治疗的惊人研究中，有很多证据表明，一旦我们刺激大脑释放、放下自我防御的面纱，移除那些组织原则和默认模式网络的屏障，一旦那层面纱被揭开，呈现的是一种开放状态——这种状态我们大多数人可能都已不记得，因为上次纯粹感受到它时，我们可能还是婴儿，对吧？或者对很多人来说是在幼儿时期。

所以我知道爱会位列其中。但关于服务和使命这两个观念，我真的很喜欢。我知道还有很多其他重要的，只是让你列出当下最重要的三条。

还有个相关的问题：你认为致幻剂在处理创伤方面有何作用？我和越来越多尝试过药物治疗路径的人交流过，有的药有效，但副作用让你不想做爱或无法达到高潮，于是你又回到谈话疗法。但情况往往是：“嗯，这周发生了这个……我不知道。”这并不……

**深入探讨更深层的话题。**

很多人没有足够的资金持续进行某种心理治疗，甚至无法维持我们许多人所需的药物治疗方案。如果某种方法无效，你就得尝试其他途径。不少人开始转向致幻剂，以此缓解他们的生存压力。人们正在尝试微量服用致幻剂，可以使用裸盖菇素、LSD 或其他物质。

此外，也有人在进行更强烈的体验。如果你读过名人分享的经历，他们会说：“我在欧亥体验了死藤水，然后生活变好了。” 但你的观点是什么？这是通往精神成长的捷径吗？是心理疗愈的捷径吗？这是否就是一些人所需要的转变？它在你所接受的医学实践中又处于什么位置？

## 致幻剂与精神连接

**安娜·尤西姆：** 我认为，致幻剂不仅为一些最难以治疗的精神和心理疾病（如酒精成瘾、抑郁、焦虑等）提供了一种新颖的神经生物学机制，对许多人而言，它们也提供了一种与精神的连接。这正是它们如此独特的原因。

在这种与精神的连接中，存在着某种与生俱来的、促进疗愈和创造完整性的力量，而这正是人们极度渴望的。通常他们正是为此而来。约翰·霍普金斯大学的许多研究已经表明，精神效应或连接越强，神秘体验越深刻，那么这次致幻之旅对个人的疗愈潜力就越大。

**迈姆·比亚利克：** 你能再说一遍吗？

**安娜·尤西姆：** 约翰·霍普金斯大学和纽约大学的研究都表明，在致幻之旅中（这里特指使用裸盖菇素，但其结论可能具有普适性），精神效应或神秘体验越强，治疗对焦虑的缓解效果就越大，或者说，对患者心理健康的好处就越大。

**乔纳森·科恩：** 你认为这是为什么？

**安娜·尤西姆：** 因为我认为，我们归根结底是生物-心理-社会-精神的复合体，是生物的、心理的、社会的，也是精神的。而人们常常会忘记精神的部分。当他们能够重新建立这种与精神的连接时，他们便开始重新感受到完整。

**迈姆·比亚利克：** 我还有一个想法，我的意思是，我们也可以用一些机制性的方式来解释其中的一部分。你是在充盈一个系统，对吧？你用血清素充盈系统，用催产素充盈系统。所以，你实际上是在为放松打下神经化学基础，对吧？这样，当你通常会陷入焦虑的轨道时，可能……（我最近没查这个领域的受体研究）……可能会有更多的受体可用于接收积极信号，而更少的受体被占用或对接来阻止这种焦虑。

所以，你是想增强效果。这正是这些东西应该发挥的作用。它们应该能增强长期的健康效果。正因如此，相比之下，保罗·斯坦茨有一个最神奇的例子。他终身为口吃所困，心灵饱受折磨。他意外地独自服用了大约五倍英雄剂量的裸盖菇素。从此他再也没有口吃过。他的整个生活仿佛被打开了，并且一直保持这种状态。

他经历了与那种连接相关的深刻精神觉醒。对大多数人来说，效果没那么显著，但这种能增强其他积极决策、其他积极体验的观念，有点像当你遇到一个人，他刚刚坠入爱河，对他而言一切都变得美妙，食物尝起来更美味，人也看起来更美。就是这么回事。这正是那种激素带给你所有的美妙感受。

**乔纳森·科恩：** 并且有可能感觉到，我们的生命存在一种精神连接，而触及这种连接是帮助我们创造出所有我们想要的神经化学“汤剂”的关键。

## 通过致幻剂疗愈

**安娜·尤西姆：** 我实际上刚刚和保罗·斯坦茨、里克·多布林一起去了南极洲的游轮。我们参加了这趟由“内幕探险”组织的游轮之旅，船上有 200 人以各种方式研究致幻剂。那是一次最有趣的经历，一次惊人的、不可思议的经历。

此外，这次旅程中有一位既是表演者又是教练的奇人，名叫布兰登，他保持着水下闭气的世界纪录。接近 30 分钟。难以置信。是的。他教我们如何在这次旅行中做到这一点。

但布兰登早前告诉了我他的故事，我想他不会介意分享。他不是我的病人或什么。他曾患有非常严重的强迫症，有一次他服用了很高剂量的 LSD，强迫症完全消失了。后来他连续六个月微量服用 LSD，彻底摆脱了强迫症。

所以，你给出的这些例子，是患有慢性终生性疾病的个体，从这些通常极难治愈的状况中获得疗愈效果。因此，这非常强大。我的意思是，这些显然是案例研究。并非人人如此，但这其中确实蕴藏着非常强大的疗愈潜力。

**迈姆·比亚利克：** 你能在水下闭气多久？

**安娜·尤西姆：** 哦，谢谢你问这个问题，迈姆。我就盼着你问呢。我刚开始大概只有两分钟。但在和布兰登训练了几天后，我几乎能达到四分半钟了，所以，提升巨大。

**迈姆·比亚利克：** 等等。我其实想问这个，因为我也在思考这个问题。有人在另一个播客里提到过。我们大多数人会想，我闭气不了那么久。对，人们通常会这么想。但事实是，这有点……我把这比作自然分娩，就像不用药物生孩子。大多数人会说，我做不到。对。但实际上，嗯，每个人都能做到。只是需要一些准备，所以你可以学会做一些看似困难、不可能等等的事情。所以这基本上与让你的系统平静下来有关。

## 呼吸训练的力量

**安娜·尤西姆：** 没错，让你的系统平静下来，并认识到，正如布兰登所说，呼吸的冲动是一种保护你的生物性驱动力，但你可以通过意志力和让自己平静下来来克服它。而且他也是那种人，他说，我能接受所有可能的结果。如果你在做那样的事情，总是有风险的，而他愿意承担风险。所以他才是保持这个纪录的完美人选。这很吸引人。他还与奥运会运动员合作，帮助他们扩大肺活量。他就是这样一个才华横溢、了不起的人。

**乔纳森·科恩：** 为什么不会造成脑损伤？

**迈姆·比亚利克：** 嗯，大脑缺氧会导致脑损伤。抱歉，我让尤西姆医生来说吧。

**乔纳森·科恩：** 是的。如果你 30 分钟不呼吸，解释一下这个机制。因为外行人会想，哦，我们一直被教导大脑需要氧气，如果你在一定时间内剥夺它的氧气，就会产生后果。这个人怎么能闭气那么长时间？是不是和那些瑜伽修行者类似，只是放慢了身体所有系统的运作？

**安娜·尤西姆：** 是的，正是如此。所以你显然需要和他谈谈，并邀请他上节目，我强烈推荐。但据我理解，这正是原因。首先，他做几项练习，通过特定的呼吸训练，在我们能力范围内最大化我们的氧合能力，他尽可能多地把氧气输送到我们的肺部。然后，他在精神和身体上教导一种放松技巧，使我们能够非常、非常深入地进入这种闭气状态。

**迈姆·比亚利克：** 你就像是进入了冬眠。基本上，你在代谢上进入了冬眠状态。所以你没有以同样的方式剥夺大脑的氧气。你也不需……

要求大脑执行任务。你越少让这种过程变得费力，就越轻松。就像如果你曾经历过严重事故，越是惊慌失措，失血速度就越快。没错，就像让系统平静下来。这与分娩并无不同。就像任何这些可能极度痛苦的处境一样。有一种重构方式，不仅能重构你的思维，还能重构你的生理状态。

**JONATHAN COHEN**：对你来说，那四分钟以上的经历是怎样的？是否有精神层面的成分，让你超越了感知到的身体极限，触及了其他东西？比如，那是什么感觉？对你来说是什么感受？

**ANNA YUSIM**：你知道，南极洲也是如此。因为我觉得在九天里，我身处一个呼吸着最洁净空气的空间，没有工业化，没有城市化。我感到与自然、与神圣、与船上这些了不起的人们紧密相连。实际上，这种感觉与其他一切相协调。

但这并不是我一直坚持的事情。所以我不知道如果今天再做，是否还能持续，但这让我觉得我真的需要和Brandon合作。这是我有一天真想投身其中并探索其潜力的事情。人类在这方面的潜力是什么？

## 禁食与精神实践

**MAYIM BIALIK**：是的，这引出了我想探讨的一个关于卡巴拉和一些特定精神传统的问题。但让我想到的是，在犹太历中，我们禁食时进行干禁食。我们有两个25小时的干禁食：一个是赎罪日（Yom Kippur），另一个在夏天的蒂沙·贝·阿夫节（Tisha B’Av）。

但人们总是问我，你是怎么做到的？一天不吃不喝怎么能活？你肯定一直饿着。我提醒人们，当你那样禁食时，你并不是像平时一样外出工作、一日三餐加两次零食。我的意思是，当我的孩子还小时，我会一边照顾他们一边禁食。但关键在于，如果你将自己置于一个代谢需求较低的环境中，你就能专注于内在而非外在事物。

我们的拉比总是教导说，你会变得像天使一样，天使不需要吃喝。那就是禁食、祈祷和冥想时的精神体验。这就是它的意义。

我想知道你是否能具体谈谈，我们时不时提到卡巴拉，我也时不时提到卡巴拉。但你能具体告诉我们，这种精神传统对你来说有什么特别之处，尤其是在你看待世界的方式和精神层面上的信息？

**ANNA YUSIM**：

是的，我对你所描述的禁食的看法与你非常相似，Mayim。这正是我在卡巴拉中学到的：我们不应关注物质食物，而应专注于内在的精神食粮。基于赎罪日25小时内的五次不同祈祷，我们有五次精神食粮，其他禁食也是如此。

我也认为，清除体内所有食物和水分，让身体得以休息，是一件非常美好、健康、美妙的事情。许多文化和传统都以强大方式利用禁食，但在我们国家却未充分利用。

例如在俄罗斯，禁食曾是消除癌症的方法，实际上是一种逆转。干禁食在这方面更为强大。但进行干禁食时，你必须身处自然环境中。干禁食期间甚至不能洗澡。关于这一点有大量书籍，也有科学依据。这在我们这里并不安全实践。通常人们会进行果汁禁食，这些也非常好且健康。

但在这个世界，毒素不断通过食物、环境侵袭我们，总的来说，疫苗和排毒是身心健康的重要组成部分，却常被忽视。

**MAYIM BIALIK**：

具体来说，你通过卡巴拉传统所学到的东西，提供了哪些关于我们精神存在的信息，或者我们作为灵性存在在这个世界中的功能？

## 主动成长与被动成长

**ANNA YUSIM**：

是的。我学到了许多重要的教训，并传递给了许多患者，也写在了书中。其中最重要的一点是，我们所有人此生都有需要学习的课程和需要克服的事情。作为一个人，我们有两种成长方式：主动成长或被动成长。

主动成长是选择成为最好的自己，能够违背天性，做更困难的事情。拥有道德勇气，正直行事，审视自己不完美的部分，并开始向相反方向转变。这就是主动成长。

凡是无法主动成长的，我们就会被动成长。那就是通过痛苦和自我折磨成长。痛苦和折磨是生命中不可避免的部分。从卡巴拉的角度看，他们认为你主动成长越多，通过痛苦和折磨被动成长的部分就越少。所以我认为这是一个非常有趣的概念。我经常与客户和患者分享这个概念，以便我们一起思考：在这种情况下，主动成长是什么样子？在这种特定情况下，违背天性是什么样子？你的默认路径是什么？成长路径又是什么？

**MAYIM BIALIK**：

我真的很喜欢这个概念，因为它将力量交到你手中，让你能够控制自己的能动性。你不能只是说，好吧，无论上帝想要什么，让我们看看会发生什么。我们需要付出努力，需要做准备工作来迎接上帝或你信仰的对象。在这段旅程中，我们与更高力量是伙伴。

我也认为，痛苦有时是机会，挑战是成长的机会。这也消除了善恶的对立，其他传统有时更强调这一点。但这个概念引入了一个事实：我们并不总是知道什么对我们好或坏，但有一条我们正在走的道路。挑战不一定被视为惩罚。不一定被视为我们做错了或搞砸了。要学习的教训是什么？成长是什么？

所以，就像每当人们问我关于祈祷的事，我最近在Substack上写了一个关于祈祷的系列，要小心你祈祷的内容。如果我祈祷我想要的东西，那就像我是上帝，知道Mayim需要什么。我通常尝试祈祷的是，对上帝对我的旨意保持开放。我不知道那是什么。如果我觉得我想要一份工作，我真的很想要那份工作。但那可能不是我道路上的东西。所以，对“如果我不快乐，这可能是一个开端”这个概念保持开放。

**ANNA YUSIM**：

我喜欢这一点。特别是认识到，我们可能认为自己想要和需要某些东西。我们可能认为我们知道什么对自己最好，但我们只看到现实的一小部分。也许存在更高的秩序，也许存在一个存在，上帝，能看到更多，并能给予我们真正需要的东西。

因为这就是此生方向性目的的理念：我们所有灵魂都以某种方式成长，我们不一定知道自己的成长路径，或者我们应该如何开放、扩展并以不同视角看待事物。所以我

非常赞同你所说的。我觉得完全正确。

## 吸引力法则

**MAYIM BIALIK:**
你谈到了吸引力法则，我们在对话开始时也大致讨论过，但从你的视角来看，你认为吸引力法则可能不奏效的理由非常有趣。意思是，为什么它不起作用呢？你说过有四种可能性。

一是你对自己所求不够清晰——有人告诉过我这点很重要。二是你内心并不真的相信自己能拥有所祈求的东西——我认为这非常有意思。三是你所祈求的可能并非对所有相关方都是最高、最伟大的善。最后一点是，当宇宙试图给予时，你却没有能力接收。

我想到有多少次我该说“是”的时候说了“不”，或者该说“不”的时候说了“是”。关于“我们准备好了接受什么”这个概念，你如何帮助我们理解它？你如何帮助你的患者、你的客户理解这个？为什么坏事总是发生在我身上？能否请你给我们一些积极的寄语，关于我们吸引什么以及我们值得拥有什么？

**ANNA YUSIM:**
当然。的确，当我们极度渴望某物却未能实现时，背后可能有多种原因。你必须深入审视内心去理解：是我的潜意识在作祟？是我的意识问题？是神的旨意？还是其他原因？

实际上，在卡巴拉中，他们提到有三种方式可以显化你内心深处最渴望的事物。其一，你在物质世界付出最大努力。也就是说，你想要某样东西，就去弄清楚如何实现它，让它发生，尽你所能。其二，你同样在精神世界付出最大努力。你祈祷，寻求帮助，努力改变自己，改变那些需要改变的部分，以成为能够接收你所需要之物的人。其三，你拥有绝对的信心。如果你能做到这三点，许多人就能得到他们想要的。这大致就是卡巴拉对此的诠释公式。

**MAYIM BIALIK:**
我特别喜欢这个说法，因为回到为什么我们有时会吸引不健康的人这个问题，我们也需要付出切实努力。我把时间花在哪里？我在阅读什么？我在消费什么？闲暇时我在做什么？我在喝酒吗？我在吸毒吗？我有规律的睡眠吗？我有照顾到营养和运动这些最基本的事情吗？因为有时候，当我们可以做很多事来帮助情况好转时，我们却总在四处寻找哪里出了错。

**ANNA YUSIM:**
完全正确。完全正确。这正是关键所在。因此，能够审视自己并问“我在这里拥有什么控制权？”，是一个关于承担责任的有力课程。认识到，尽你所能去控制你能控制的，然后意识到对于大多数最重要的事情，控制权在某种程度上是有限的，并且在某种程度上，你还必须学会臣服，并敞开心扉去接收可能比你自己以为的所求对你更有利的事物。

**MAYIM BIALIK:**
Yusim 医生，真的非常感谢你今天来到这里。我强烈推荐《Fulfilled: How the Science of Spirituality Can Help You Live a Happier, More Meaningful Life》这本书。我们还没机会讨论书里那些不可思议的冥想和练习。所以 Jonathan 和我会去 Substack 上分享一些书中实用内容的例子。

这本书真的……它确实是一本非常精彩的书。它是对你独特而个人旅程的美好探索。我特别喜欢听到关于患者的故事以及你帮助她们取得的进步。但此外，这也是一本非常实用的书，我也很欣赏这一点。非常感谢你与我们进行这场对话。

**ANNA YUSIM:**
谢谢大家。这真是太棒了。谢谢。

## 解读第三只眼

**JONATHAN COHEN:**
我现在正在解读你的第三只眼。

**MAYIM BIALIK:**
我真想在第三只眼上贴个创可贴到处走，这样就没人能解读它了。

**JONATHAN COHEN:**
人们会直接告诉你你在想什么、感受什么。

**MAYIM BIALIK:**
他们甚至不需要看到它。问题就在这儿。我周围都是像你和她这样的人，他们总是在窥探我的大脑。

**JONATHAN COHEN:**
我们得教你如何解读自己的顶轮。我们得扩展你的意识。这是什么时候的事？有时候，做太多播客的问题就在于，我记得我们有过一场关于改变意识的对话。我其实觉得那是在一次 Substack 直播中，我试图引导你进行冥想。

那个冥想的核心内容与 Anna 所说的相关，即我们可以将意识调整到一个频率或一个位置，超越我们自身的理解，进入一个更宏大的、宇宙性的理解。你当时对此非常生气。

**MAYIM BIALIK:**
哦，我告诉过你。你真是疯了。

**JONATHAN COHEN:**
Yusim 医生可能会说，你知道吗，Mayim，清理一下自己。提起你的觉知。

**MAYIM BIALIK:**
你不知道她会跟我说什么。也别钻进她脑子里。我阻止不了你。你到处在进行心灵冲浪。你无处不在。

**JONATHAN COHEN:**
如果有听众想让我进入他们的脑袋，告诉我一声。我很乐意四处看看，瞧瞧我能看到什么。

**MAYIM BIALIK:**
不，不过说真的，老实说，我现在看待这些事情的眼光不同了，因为我想到了有时在我的瑜伽课上看到的那些人。或者他们走在我们中间，你能看出这些人联结着某种不同的东西。但他们的生活似乎并不总是按我认为应该的方式进行。他们四处走动时，可能对自己接收到的信息感到非常不堪重负。这快把我逼疯了。我不知道该如何理解这件事。

## 驾驭超感官信息

**JONATHAN COHEN:**
嗯，你提出了一个重要观点，因为人们想要为自己获取信息，但这确实是你必须学会驾驭的东西。当你开始从四种“克莱尔”能力之一接收超感官信息时，可能会让人非常迷失方向。你不知道该关注什么。有些人边界感不好。他们的过滤器开得太宽，你只会感到不堪重负。当你周围的每个人都在用信息淹没你时，你怎么能只是去趟杂货店呢？

**MAYIM BIALIK:**
你的过滤器开得太宽了。这就是我想对人们说的。从我的第三只眼里出去。你的过滤器开得太宽了。

**JONATHAN COHEN:**
你只需要专注于自己的意识。

**MAYIM BIALIK:**
你知道我在想什么吗？首先，我们多么喜欢做 Substack 直播。因为我知道，当我们为本周播出的这期节目做 Substack 直播时，正在收听的你们，大家会以最好的方式激动不已。

还想提一下，大家可以访问 annayusim.com 了解她的精神病学诊疗和教练服务。我感觉我需要见她。我希望她成为我的人。我希望她告诉我所有事情。解读我的第三只眼。

**JONATHAN COHEN:**
她总是在我们说完话后停顿很久。

**MAYIM BIALIK:**
非常有耐心的女人。

**JONATHAN COHEN:**
我不确定是不是网络问题，但其实不是。

**MAYIM BIALIK:**
她是精神科医生。他们受过训练要那样做。

**JONATHAN COHEN:**
你知道为什么要停顿吗？如果你匆匆忙忙，就无法接收到四种“克莱尔”能力之一。

**MAYIM BIALIK:**
哦，天啊。这是你的新昵称，克莱尔。从我们的《Breakdown》到我们希望你们永远不会有的崩溃。下次见，我和克莱尔。

**JONATHAN COHEN:**
这里是 Mayim Bialik 的《Breakdown》。

**ANNA YUSIM:**
她会为你剖析明白。

**JONATHAN COHEN:**
她有一个或两个神经科学博士学位，现在她要开始剖析了。

**ANNA YUSIM:**
这就是《Breakdown》。

**乔纳森·科恩：** 她会进行详细解析。

### 相关文章
articles.notebooklm_summary:
这篇文章是播客《比亚利克深度解析》（Mayim Bialik's Breakdown）一期节目的详细摘要，主持人迈伊姆·比亚利克（Mayim Bialik）与乔纳森·科恩（Jonathan Cohen）采访了耶鲁大学精神科医生安娜·尤西姆博士（Dr. Anna Yusim）。

以下是关于该对话的详细中文摘要，涵盖了主要观点、关键信息和结论：

### 1. 对话背景与核心主题
本期节目探讨了**西方医学（精神病学）与灵性智慧的交叉点**。尤西姆博士作为一名受过耶鲁和斯坦福训练的精神科医生，分享了她如何将灵性科学融入临床实践，以帮助患者超越传统药物治疗的局限，解决深层的心理和生活问题 [1-3]。

### 2. 主要观点与关键信息

#### **A. 灵性与科学的融合**
*   **误解与现实**：人们常认为科学（经验主义、可触摸）与灵性（主观、不可复现）是对立的。尤西姆博士认为两者紧密相连 [4]。
*   **科学证据**：研究表明灵性实践能改善心理健康。例如，定期去教堂可显著降低自杀率。虽然社区归属感是因素之一，但研究发现核心因素是宗教灌输的“反对自杀的道德禁忌” [5, 6]。
*   **宗教 vs. 灵性**：社会趋势显示，有组织的宗教在减少，而个人化的灵性追求在增加。人们渴望通过冥想、瑜伽或自然连接神圣，而非依赖教条 [7]。

#### **B. 直觉与“四种超感官知觉” (The 4 Clairs)**
尤西姆博士定义了接收直觉信息的四种方式，统称为“Clairs” [8-10]：
1.  **灵视 (Clairvoyance)**：在脑海中看到图像或画面。
2.  **灵听 (Clairaudience)**：听到声音或讯息（需与精神分裂症区分，后者通常具有破坏性且无法控制，而健康的直觉是建设性的）[11]。
3.  **灵觉 (Clairsentience)**：身体上的感觉或情感上的认知（Gut feeling）。
4.  **灵知 (Claircognizance)**：一种毫无理由的“就是知道”的确信感。

*   **1%与99%的现实**：借用卡巴拉的概念，我们感官能感知的（视觉、听觉）仅占现实的1%，其余99%是不可见的能量和意识场 [12]。

#### **C. 潜意识、创伤与代际模式**
*   **自我破坏**：许多人不仅在意识层面努力，却因潜意识中的核心信念而自我破坏（如渴望伴侣却总是吸引情感不可得的人）。弗洛伊德称之为“强迫性重复”，尤西姆称之为“灵魂修正”（Soul Correction） [13, 14]。
*   **代际创伤**：创伤和行为模式不仅是心理的，也会在能量层面通过家族遗传。通过家庭系统排列（Family Constellations）和意识到这些模式，个体可以打破循环，停止向后代传递创伤 [15-17]。

#### **D. 提升频率与显化 (Manifestation)**
*   **两种显化方式**：
    1.  **阳性/行动导向**：设定目标，努力工作达成。
    2.  **阴性/存在导向**：提升自身的振动频率（Joy, Peace），通过“臣服”来吸引与该频率匹配的事物 [18]。
*   **提升频率的方法**：清除降低频率的负面情绪（如愤怒、嫉妒），并用积极的体验（感恩、服务）填充自己。这实际上是从“收缩”状态转向“扩展”状态 [19, 20]。

#### **E. 灵性工具与治疗手段**
*   **通灵与媒介**：尤西姆博士承认通灵者（Mediums）的存在，他们能读取信息。她区分了读取“第三眼”（反映你自己的直觉和投射）与读取“顶轮”（连接神圣或更高维度的信息）的区别 [21, 22]。
*   **致幻剂治疗**：如裸盖菇素（Psilocybin）在治疗成瘾和抑郁中的作用。研究表明，致幻体验中的“神秘体验”越强烈，疗愈效果越好，因为它帮助人们重新连接失落的灵性部分 [23, 24]。
*   **呼吸法**：通过特定的呼吸训练（如Wim Hof式）可以平复神经系统，甚至让人做到超越常理的事（如水下闭气4分钟），这展示了身心控制的潜力 [25, 26]。

### 3. 结论与普世真理

尤西姆博士总结了跨越不同传统的三个普世灵性真理 [27]：
1.  **爱的普遍性**：爱自己、爱他人、爱神圣存在。
2.  **服务的重要性**：通过奉献和帮助他人来超越小我。
3.  **使命 (Purpose)**：找到你来到这个世界的独特目的。

**最终总结**：
心理健康不仅是生物和心理的，也是灵性的。通过结合科学的理性（如利弊分析）与灵性的直觉（如臣服与指引），人们可以从“被动成长”（通过痛苦学习）转向“主动成长”（通过选择和觉知学习），从而过上更圆满、更具意义的生活 [28, 29]。

articles.raw_text:
# Ajay Banga on India, Migration and a Youth Jobs Time Bomb (Transcript)

Editor’s Notes: In this insightful episode of Leaders with Francine Lacqua, World Bank President Ajay Banga discusses the pressing “youth jobs time bomb” and the demographic shift reshaping emerging markets. Banga shares how his upbringing in India and decades of leadership at global giants like Mastercard and Citigroup have informed his mission to bridge the gap between talent and opportunity. He emphasizes the critical role of private sector investment and long-term thinking in tackling poverty, migration, and the unique challenges of the digital age. This conversation offers a rare look at the man steering one of the world’s most influential institutions through a period of historic economic transformation. (Feb 15, 2026)

TRANSCRIPT:

AJAY BANGA: In the coming 15 years, the emerging markets have what’s called a demographic boom coming through them. We just want to give them hope and aspiration. Because if you don’t, the alternative is mass migration, is fragility, conflict and violence.

FRANCINE LACQUA: And uprisings and all. So how long are you in London for today?

AJAY BANGA: Two days. Just today. And then doing a couple of days, the Philippines and then to Tokyo and then off to China and then back home.

FRANCINE LACQUA: You’re never tired.

AJAY BANGA: You get used to it over the years.

FRANCINE LACQUA: Do you?

AJAY BANGA: Yeah, I’ve done 24 hour trips to Australia and back and people like, you’re crazy. I know. It’s actually great because you’re in no time zone.

FRANCINE LACQUA: Yeah. But it’s tough, it’s tough on the body. How long have you been doing this? Like 20 years.

AJAY BANGA: 1999. 98. 99.

## The World Bank’s Mission

FRANCINE LACQUA: The mission for the World Bank is basically end poverty in a livable planet.

AJAY BANGA: Correct. Which is exactly jobs and quality of life.

FRANCINE LACQUA: Ajay Banga runs the World Bank and he spends a lot of his time thinking about one impending problem. The World Bank estimates that 1.2 billion people are expected to enter the job market over the next decade. But there will only be 400 million new jobs then.

So what will happen to the 800 million youth for whom no job exists? This is a ticking time bomb of historic proportions. By 2050, more than 80% of the world population will live in what today we consider developing countries. And these economies will hold the vast majority of young people with the largest growth in urban populations.

What made you decide to go to the World Bank?

AJAY BANGA: I just believe that the world’s problems are thick and dense and there are two or three interlocking problems. The problem, inequality and poverty. The really serious challenge of the fact that talent is everywhere but opportunity and capital is not, is a big issue. And that affects gender, it affects people of ethnic systems, it affects everything.

And the problem is anything you do on these issues of poverty and a better quality of life for people requires long term thinking. And unfortunately, the system is incented in short term thinking. Whether you’re a politician or you’re a teacher or you’re a CEO, the system pushes you towards less than optimal time frames.

## Long-Term Thinking at the World Bank

FRANCINE LACQUA: The World Bank is where long term thinking thrives. It’s a very big, slow moving bureaucratic machine where credits and loans can have final maturities of up to 50 years. And it employs over 20,000 people across more than 140 locations. It’s 80 years old.

AJAY BANGA: Four long years of intensive work have gone into laying the groundwork for this.

FRANCINE LACQUA: Day, it’s an intergovernmental institution made up of 189 member countries. It financed bullet trains in Japan and rebuilt the country after World War II. It also loaned $250 million to France in 1947. India, China, Indonesia, Nigeria and Vietnam are just a few other economies the World Bank has helped.

AJAY BANGA: I knew it’s going to be tough. I knew it’s going to be cumbersome. I knew it’s going to be part of a bureaucracy. But bureaucracies exist in the private sector, too. Change is something you’ve got to do everywhere. And so that doesn’t scare me. But the idea of being able to make a difference and not be an armchair critic, that inspires me.

## Growing Up in India

FRANCINE LACQUA: You also grew up looking at people in poverty. Did that shape your worldview from a very young age?

AJAY BANGA: When I was young and growing up in India, I lived a sheltered life because of the army. But the moment you go out and go for a holiday or you go on your way to school every day and you go on a school bus, you see everything. And then you realize that there’s another thing out there.

I didn’t understand, quite honestly, till I was much older, the aspect this meant and what that differentiation really meant. I didn’t understand it. I was probably in my high school days and I began to understand what it meant to those people and what it meant to their life and how fortunate I was to be where I was then. Of course, it does make a difference.

My father was in the army, which meant we sort of moved every couple of years. And army brat is what they get called. So one of the quick things you learn is how to fit into a school and make friends.

FRANCINE LACQUA: But you have to be pretty adaptable. Is adaptability one of the things that is necessary now to be a good leader?

AJAY BANGA: Both adaptability and affability, you got to kind of get along because you are the one who comes from outside. Also the willingness to not always want what you want, but understand that others want to do things too. And you’ve got to have curiosity, you’ve got to have adjustability, you’ve got to have a level of affability. You’ve got to have all that to get along.

FRANCINE LACQUA: Were you always a good negotiator?

AJAY BANGA: I guess that comes with the territory.

## Building a Career Defined by Scale

FRANCINE LACQUA: Ajay Banga went on to study economics at Delhi University and a post grad at the Indian Institute of Management, Ahmedabad.

And he rose through the ranks at Citigroup to oversee one of the bank’s largest regions. By 2008, Ajay Banga was appointed chief Executive officer of Citigroup Asia Pacific. And he brought modern banking to millions.

AJAY BANGA: I worked in India my first few years after my MBA were in sales in Nestle. And they basically put you out there in these towns and villages doing a 26 day and a month routine of travel with the sales rep and a salesman from a local distributor in different towns.

You’re traveling in buses and small jeeps and living in small hotels and really not $2 a day. That’s what my daily allowance used to be, which was a fair amount in India and the rural part of India at that time. But it just tells you, you suddenly get this exposure and you realize that the products you’re selling cost a lot compared to what others can afford. And there’s a whole awareness that breathes like osmosis into your skin.

The period at Citi is when I realized that what I had learned, I’d seen when I was growing up, you could use traditional financial institutions, their skills, their capacity and their capability, and make a difference to these others who in turn were impacting people whom I had seen and never thought I could do something about. But now I found I could do my business and also do this. And that became kind of the idea of doing good and doing well at the same time. And people call it doing well by doing good, doing good by doing well. It’s very confusing, but the idea is you can do both.

## The MasterCard Years

FRANCINE LACQUA: Banga joined MasterCard next as chief operating officer and went on to become president and chief executive officer. With an eight figure pay package, Banga pushed MasterCard beyond traditional payments into the digital economy.

AJAY BANGA: I want to find a way to kill cash. That’s what I thought the opportunity was in MasterCard that was financial inclusion because so many people were in cash and if you wanted them to get included and then start using digital payments, that is an automatic reason to do so, right? And yet that is daunting because it is so embedded in lives.

And it is in countries like India, it was 90% of transactions. Even in America at that time, it was 65, 70% of transactions. And so it was very embedded. It was a big thing to take on. We started breaking it down into what you could do. It came to a very simple strategy. Grow your existing business, credit, debit, prepaid, that stuff. Diversify your clients to not just the biggest banks, but also add smaller banks, fintechs, technology firms, governments do all that and then build out new businesses.

Which was the company MasterCard at that time, but 4% of its revenues used to come from data analytics, what’s now called AI, right? And I basically said, we’ll get that to 40% by the time 10 years go by. And when I left, you were actually at 36%. Let’s take humility, put it in a box and throw it out the window.

FRANCINE LACQUA: You’re now CEO of MasterCard. Citi had you on deck to be CEO of Citi. You are a big deal.

AJAY BANGA: Where’s my daughter? Is she in the audience? When I joined MasterCard, it was a tiny company. It was $20 billion market cap by the time I left to $360 billion.

FRANCINE LACQUA: And what do you remember as most difficult at MasterCard? You grew it immensely. What was it? Getting the people to follow your leadership or just convincing clients that you were the go to?

## Leadership and Technology Transformation

AJAY BANGA: Yeah, no, there were multiple challenges. One was the whole technology was changing around you. And if you go back to when I first joined, there, it was in 2009. Digitization had arrived. But think of the change between 2009 and 2020 and that dramatic change and the adoption of phones and biometric ways of paying and wallets on a phone, and the growth of an Apple and the growth of a Google and the growth of Alipay in the east and China and the like, all that is going on during that period.

So technology and the change actually gave me the chance to tell people that if you don’t adjust, you will become extinct. And the way to stay ahead of this is to embrace the change and make it part of your culture.

Leadership is a privilege. It’s not your birthright. And so you’ve got to think about how you can help others grow. And that’s what management is all about, right? When I was younger, it was all about your IQ. If you’re smart, you kind of did well. And by the time you got to business school, it became about your EQ. We were being taught that you had to get along with people and you couldn’t really choose the boss you worked with or the colleagues. And you had to find a way to adjust and adapt.

But now I think it’s about your DQ, which is your decency quotient, which is, can you actually get people to realize that your hand is on their back? You’re pushing them, but it’s on the back. It’s not on their face. You’re not giving them just a free handout. You’re not just trying to give them a starting point that’s equal and then let the best one win.

FRANCINE LACQUA: When do you have to decide whether that’s the person you should be? Is it something that you’re taught at school or is it in your first job?

AJAY BANGA: You pick it up over time. Just kind of pick it up over time. It’s osmosis. I just believe that one of the best things about a person is osmosis. If you’re curious, you will have osmosis. If you lose your curiosity, you’re done. Fail fast.

FRANCINE LACQUA: I like that. How do you fail fast? Do you implement fast? And then if it doesn’t work, you move?

AJAY BANGA: You try stuff. If it doesn’t work, you’ve got to know how to cut the cord. The worst thing you can do is you know it’s not working. And you keep trying to figure out, I’m so smart, I must have thought of this the right way. I just need to do another trick in it. At some point, you have to learn that that’s not going to work, stop and move on to the next one. And I think that comes from that same openness. Are you listening to people? Are you listening to what they’re saying?

## The Future of Youth Employment

FRANCINE LACQUA: When you leave the bank, what do you want?

AJAY BANGA: I want young people around the world of whom right now there’s 1.2 billion coming through the pipe in the emerging markets to be ready for a job. Those young people, they can drive the next 30, 40 years of growth and development, but they have to have a chance. While you look at the last three years, look at a number of countries around the world and you’ll see the youngest people are in the streets protesting.

## The Gen Z Jobs Crisis

This flag pops up in nearly all these protests. It’s not just a pirate emblem. It’s a symbol of freedom, resistance, defiance, and the pursuit of dreams. Gen Z are setting new records on the misery index, an economic indicator of unhappiness equal to the sum of inflation and unemployment rate. Gen Z protesters collapsed governments in Madagascar, Nepal, Bulgaria and Bangladesh.

In short, Gen Z are not all right. They’re living a job squeeze of historic proportions. They’re pessimistic. For some, corruption is rampant, equality inexistent. Others lack basic public services and infrastructure. Globally, one in four young people are neither employed nor studying.

Today, my friends, everyone calls me Emmy and I’m 23 years old. I’m from Kathmandu. I was working as a freelancer. Answer MC Nepal is still an underdeveloping nation. The way a government has implemented its resources, we have been facing unemployment very badly. We could support ourselves with small freelancing gigs from the social media. And YouTube was kind of our school away from school. Social media is also a place to put up your frustration.

AJAY BANGA: So when government banned social media, it felt like it’s curtailing our voice.

FRANCINE LACQUA: So of course it ignited an anger in everyone. It was an entire generation were frustrated with the way things were, with the unemployment, with the corruption cases and everything. So it was like there were collectively different sort of groups around, scattered. But things quickly escalated and violence broke out.

It was a very brutal scene. The place where carried so much of beautiful memories of my life turn into a war. John. It unfold in front of my eyes and it just, I could not process and I could not believe that, okay, I’m actually witnessing this. All I could hear was nonsense, stop gunshots. Everyone were like, okay, we are done. We’re done with these people.

The parliament was on fire. Ministers were thrashed on the streets. The Supreme Court was torched and the capital of Kathmandu was overrun. It was like the country, whole country had lost its sanity. And in less than 48 hours after the Prime Minister resigned, our country went into the parliament power vacuum.

For the next three days, a victory would be a good, fresh parliament where I would see deserving, promising faces and who would take up all this mandate from our revolution, from our protest. A whole generations have been awake and aware and questioning, seeking accountability that in the long term would be considered a victory. If you look at the necessary jobs in the future, with AI and the number of young people coming to the workforce, there’s a mismatch.

## AI’s Different Impact on Emerging Markets

AJAY BANGA: Well, here’s the thing. In the emerging markets, AI is different from what you see, the opportunity or threats of AI in the developed world. Because big AI, which is what we talk about in the developed world, requires a few things. It requires lots of computing power, lots of electricity, lots of data, and lots of people who understand how to use that.

There are very few emerging markets that have those four things. And so actually what you’ll end up happening in the emerging markets will be small AI, which is tools and applications delivered at the edge on a phone that isn’t necessarily the world’s smartest phone, which helps you with health care and farming tips and education.

FRANCINE LACQUA: But if we stay as we are, there’s still not enough jobs for the use of today.

AJAY BANGA: Well, look at it this way. What are the opportunities in the emerging markets to create jobs? If you look, there are five sectors. The first one is infrastructure. The second one is agriculture. The third one is tourism. So if you think of the opportunities of tourism in these countries and tourism creates the most jobs per dollar invested, and the fourth is health care. And the last one is value added manufacturing locally, whether it’s pharma or minerals and metals, none of those are AI impacted as much as you would think they would be.

FRANCINE LACQUA: No, but it means that you need to have a stable politics. Right, Politics. You need growth.

AJAY BANGA: Yes.

FRANCINE LACQUA: And you need job creation.

AJAY BANGA: You need three things to happen. You need the infrastructure, you need the right governance and policies. That’s the point you’re making. And the third is you need the private sector because jobs are created in the private sector.

## The Role of Private Capital

FRANCINE LACQUA: The world needs trillions of dollars over the next decade to address climate change, infrastructure gaps, food insecurity and energy transitions. But there simply isn’t enough public money in the world for that. Private capital, on the other hand, is much more readily available. And about 90% of jobs in developing economies are in the private sector.

AJAY BANGA: But even in Ukraine, the private sector is alive and well. It’s just different from the private sector we think about. But if you see Ukraine and you look at the private sector there, the World Bank is actually financing trade, finance, and they’re putting money into insurance companies. And there is a private sector in technology, in drone manufacturing, in all that.

So every country has something going on. The question is, can you encourage that private sector, through catalytic capital, to go further? You can never do that without the right governance and policies and the right infrastructure. It’s absolutely correct. But you can’t solve every country, but you can solve 20. You made a difference, the lives of the millions of people in those 20.

And that’s the kind of focus we are on. Work on infrastructure, work on right governance and policies. Because we are a knowledge bank, not just a money bank. We can actually help with that and then work with the private sector and do it in those five sectors. I’m talking about either as an entrepreneur or as a job working for somebody else. That’s the pivot the World Bank is on.

FRANCINE LACQUA: But if someone comes to you and says, what’s in it for me? What’s your answer?

AJAY BANGA: What’s in it for you in a country is the fact that if you can help to grow the economy overseas and create jobs, your companies benefit, your products benefit, your technology benefits, and most importantly, you ensure that there are stable systems and stable societies and stable markets overseas. The number one political issue of our time, the crisis of uncontrolled migration. It’s uncontrolled.

## Migration and Populism

FRANCINE LACQUA: Doesn’t populism teach you actually that you shouldn’t care what happens outside your borders? You just don’t want anyone in?

AJAY BANGA: Yeah, you shouldn’t. But the question is, there’s only one form of populism, real form of populism, when you think through what people are discussing, is that you want opportunities for your people. And the only way you’ll get opportunities for your people is actually through global markets and global systems. Because you have companies and technology and products and services that are advantaged. I think that’s just worth keeping in your mind.

What will tear us apart is illegal migration. So when you think about what’s going on in our world and what is making people feel insecure, yes, AI is a problem. But if you think about illegal migration, not migration, we all need legal migration. Because we don’t have all the skills or all the people in the older societies of the developed world. Legal migration is really important and should be encouraged in the right way. Illegal migration can be a problem.

FRANCINE LACQUA: Every day someone says multilateralism is dead.

AJAY BANGA: Yes.

FRANCINE LACQUA: Do you agree with that?

## The Future of Multilateralism

AJAY BANGA: No. Because during the last year or two, when things were really tough, IDA 21 happened. That’s the, we got to raise money every three years for that part of the bank that goes to 78 poorest countries. Because we give away one third of the money every year without repayment. So it kind of runs out even though we are profitable and the rest of the bank runs on its own. But this one requires recapitalization.

During this entire period, we recapitalized IDA21. That is the campaign at a record level, higher than ever earlier. And that was during the time period that the Japanese government changed, the German government changed, the French government changed a couple of times, the British government changed, the American government changed, the Canadian government changed. The Korean government declared martial law on the day of our event in Seoul.

So even with political turmoil, there is a role for multilateralism. The question is what kind of multilateralism aimed for what? And that, I think, is the issue.

FRANCINE LACQUA: And so how has it changed? This is what, public money that gets married also with private money?

AJAY BANGA: Yes, absolutely. So that’s a really important point that you will find that fiscal constraints exist everywhere in the world. The developed world has…

FRANCINE LACQUA: Debt is rising.

AJAY BANGA: And therefore relying on handouts is not going to work. I really believe in any case, that handouts have a low outcome probability and that you have to find a way to incent people to use the money they’re getting, to use it for better. It’s not that you don’t need some handouts, but you do need those grants. But you do need as well, the incentive to use that money to make things happen.

And I think the private sector and private capital mobilization combined with the money you can get from the public sector combined with philanthropy, that’s a good combination to work with. My kids, by the way, had no idea what the World Bank was when I first told them. They were like, what are you talking about, dad? What’s the World Bank? I thought you worked at Citibank and MasterCard. What’s this?

## Personal Values and Leadership

FRANCINE LACQUA: How many children you have?

AJAY BANGA: I got two girls. I want my daughters to never think that their father was an armchair critic. That would be a tragic sort of thing to be.

FRANCINE LACQUA: What’s the one thing that you constantly told them growing up?

AJAY BANGA: That you can beat any boy.

FRANCINE LACQUA: That’s a good motto. Are you different in your private life than you are at work? Do you like to keep those separate?

AJAY BANGA: I have a very simple rule. When I’m finished at work in the evening, if I’m out of the family, I actually don’t take my phone along and I leave at home. And I’ve been doing this for years, and it allows me to just switch off because I’m in the moment for where I am. And I’m here with you right now, and I care about what I’m talking about to you and with my kids. It’s them.

FRANCINE LACQUA: Ajay, thank you so much.

AJAY BANGA: Thank you. Thanks a lot.

# 阿贾伊·邦加谈印度、人口迁移与青年就业定时炸弹（文字实录）

**编者按**：在本期《Leaders with Francine Lacqua》节目中，世界银行行长阿贾伊·邦加深入探讨了迫在眉睫的“青年就业定时炸弹”以及重塑新兴市场的人口结构转变。邦加分享了他童年时代在印度的成长经历，以及他在万事达卡和花旗集团等全球巨头数十年领导生涯中的体悟，这些经历如何促使他致力于弥合人才与机遇间的鸿沟。他强调私营部门投资和长远思维在应对贫困、人口迁移及数字时代独特挑战方面的关键作用。本次访谈让我们难得一窥这位正引领全球最具影响力的机构之一度过历史性经济转型期的领导者的思考。

**文字实录：**

**阿贾伊·邦加：** 未来15年，新兴市场将迎来所谓的人口激增。我们只想给他们希望和抱负。因为如果你不这样做，替代选择将会是大规模迁移、社会脆弱、冲突和暴力。

**弗朗辛·拉夸：** 还有起义之类的。那么你今天在伦敦待多久？

**阿贾伊·邦加：** 两天。今天就结束了。然后会去菲律宾几天，接着去东京，然后去中国，最后回家。

**弗朗辛·拉夸：** 你从不觉得累。

**阿贾伊·邦加：** 这么多年下来你就习惯了。

**弗朗辛·拉夸：** 是吗？

**阿贾伊·邦加：** 是的，我试过24小时内往返澳大利亚，别人觉得我疯了。我知道。但其实很棒，因为你不用倒时差。

**弗朗辛·拉夸：** 是啊。但这很辛苦，对身体负担很大。你这样多久了？大概20年吧。

**阿贾伊·邦加：** 从1999年。98、99年左右开始。

## 世界银行的使命

**弗朗辛·拉夸：** 世界银行的使命基本就是在宜居的星球上消除贫困。

**阿贾伊·邦加：** 没错。这恰恰就是工作和生活质量的问题。

**弗朗辛·拉夸：** 阿贾伊·邦加掌管世界银行，他将大量时间用来思考一个迫在眉睫的问题。世界银行估计，未来十年将有12亿人进入就业市场。但届时只会新增4亿个工作岗位。

那么，对于那8亿没有工作的年轻人来说，将会发生什么？这是一颗具有历史意义的定时炸弹。到2050年，超过80%的世界人口将居住在今天我们所谓的发展中国家。而这些经济体将拥有绝大多数的年轻人，其城市人口也将经历最大增长。

是什么让你决定去世界银行工作？

**阿贾伊·邦加：** 我只是相信世界上的问题盘根错节，有两三个相互交织的问题。这个问题就是不平等与贫困。人才无处不在，但机会和资本却非如此，这个严峻的挑战是个大问题。这影响到性别问题，影响到不同族裔的人，影响到方方面面。

问题是，任何针对贫困和为人们创造更好生活质量这些议题的行动，都需要长远思考。而不幸的是，现行制度倾向于短期思维。无论你是政治家、教师还是CEO，制度都把你推向不那么理想的时间框架。

## 世界银行的长远思维

**弗朗辛·拉夸：** 世界银行是长远思维得以滋长的地方。它是一个庞大、缓慢运转的官僚机器，其信贷和贷款的最终期限可达50年之久。它在全球140多个地点雇佣了超过2万名员工。它已有80年历史。

**阿贾伊·邦加：** 为此，我们已经投入了四年漫长而紧张的工作来奠定基础。

**弗朗辛·拉夸：** 它是一个由189个成员国组成的政府间机构。它曾为日本的新干线提供资金，并在二战后帮助重建国家。它还在1947年向法国贷款2.5亿美元。印度、中国、印度尼西亚、尼日利亚和越南只是世界银行援助过的众多经济体中的几个例子。

**阿贾伊·邦加：** 我知道这将会很艰难。我知道这会很繁琐。我知道这是官僚机构的一部分。但私营部门也存在官僚主义。变革是你必须在各处都要进行的事情。所以这吓不到我。但能够有所作为而非仅仅是纸上谈兵，这个想法激励着我。

## 在印度的成长经历

**弗朗辛·拉夸：** 你成长过程中也看到了贫困中的人们。这是否在很早就塑造了你的世界观？

**阿贾伊·邦加：** 我年轻时在印度长大，由于军队背景，我过着一种受庇护的生活。但一旦你出门度假，或者每天上学路上、坐上校车，你就什么都看到了。然后你意识到外面还有另一个世界。

说实话，直到年龄大很多后，我才真正理解这意味着什么，以及那种差异的真正含义。那时我不明白。大概是在高中时期，我开始理解这对那些人意味着什么，对他们的生活意味着什么，以及我能处于当时的位置是多么幸运。当然，这确实带来了不同。

我父亲在军队服役，这意味着我们大约每两年就要搬一次家。他们管这叫“军队小鬼”。所以你很快要学会的一件事就是如何适应学校并交朋友。

**弗朗辛·拉夸：** 但你必须相当善于适应。适应性现在是成为优秀领导者的必要条件之一吗？

**阿贾伊·邦加：** 适应性和亲和力两者都需要，你必须与人相处融洽，因为你是外来者。还要愿意并不总是坚持己见，而是理解他人也有想做的事情。你必须有好奇心，必须有适应能力，必须有亲和力。你必须具备所有这些才能与人相处。

**弗朗辛·拉夸：** 你一直是个好的谈判者吗？

**阿贾伊·邦加：** 我想这是环境使然。

## 职业生涯的规模化发展

**弗朗辛·拉夸：** 阿贾伊·邦加随后在德里大学攻读经济学，并在印度管理研究所艾哈迈达巴德分校攻读研究生课程。

他在花旗集团步步晋升，负责管理该银行最大的区域之一。到2008年，阿贾伊·邦加被任命为花旗集团亚太区首席执行官。他将现代银行业务带给数百万人。

**阿贾伊·邦加：** MBA毕业后的头几年我在印度的雀巢公司做销售工作。他们基本上把你派到这些城镇和村庄，跟着销售代表和来自当地分销商的销售员，在不同的城镇度过每月26天的出差行程。

你乘坐巴士和小吉普车旅行，住在小旅馆里，真的不是一天2美元。那是我当时的每日津贴，在当时印度的乡村地区，这是一笔不小的数目。但这让你明白，你突然接触到这些，并意识到你销售的产品价格相对于他人能负担得起的来说非常昂贵。一种意识仿佛通过渗透作用进入你的皮肤。

在花旗集团的时期，我意识到，我可以运用我所学到的知识、我成长过程中的所见所闻，利用传统金融机构的技能、产能和能力，去影响那些进而影响我曾见过、却从未想过我能做些什么的人们。但现在我发现，我可以在经营好业务的同时也做这件事。这就成了“做好事也做好事”的理念。人们称之为“通过做好事来做好事，通过做好事来做好事”。这很令人困惑，但理念是你可以两者兼顾。

## 万事达卡的岁月

**弗朗辛·拉夸：** 随后，邦加加入万事达卡担任首席运营官，并后来成为总裁兼首席执行官。凭借八位数的薪酬，邦加推动万事达卡超越传统支付领域，进军数字经济。

**阿贾伊·邦加：** 我想找到一种消灭现金的方法。那就是……

我认为当时万事达卡（MasterCard）的机遇在于普惠金融——因为当时大多数人仍使用现金，若想让他们融入数字支付体系，这便是一个天然的切入点，对吗？然而这也令人望而生畏，因为它已深深嵌入日常生活。

在印度这样的国家，现金交易占比高达90%。即便在当时美国，现金交易也占65%至70%。它如此根深蒂固，改变并非易事。我们开始分解可行的步骤，最终形成极其简单的战略：发展现有业务（信用卡、借记卡、预付费卡等）；拓展客户范围，不仅服务大银行，也纳入小银行、金融科技公司、科技企业和政府机构；同时开拓新业务。

当时的万事达卡虽已是成熟企业，但其收入中仅有4%来自数据分析业务（即如今所说的人工智能）。而我当时明确提出：我们要在十年内将这个比例提升至40%。当我离任时，实际已达到36%——不过还是让我们把自负塞进盒子扔出窗外吧。

**弗朗辛·拉克瓦：** 您现在已是万事达卡CEO。花旗集团曾将您列为CEO人选。您确实举足轻重。

**阿贾伊·班加：** 我女儿在哪儿？她在观众席吗？我加入万事达卡时，它还是个小公司。市值从我入职时的200亿美元增长到我离任时的3600亿美元。

**弗朗辛·拉克瓦：** 您在万事达卡期间最难忘的困难是什么？您让公司实现了巨大增长。是让团队追随您的领导，还是说服客户选择贵司？

## 领导力与技术转型

**阿贾伊·班加：** 确实面临多重挑战。其一，整个技术环境正在剧变。回想2009年我刚加入时，数字化浪潮已然到来。但纵观2009至2020年间，变革何其剧烈——手机普及、生物识别支付、手机钱包兴起，苹果、谷歌崛起，东方支付宝等平台蓬勃发展……这一切都发生在那段时期。

技术变革给了我契机去告诫人们：若不适应，终将被淘汰。保持领先的唯一方式就是拥抱变化，将其融入企业文化。

领导力是一种特权，而非与生俱来的权利。因此必须思考如何助力他人成长——这正是管理的核心。年轻时人们只重智商，聪明人往往表现优异；到了商学院，情商成为焦点，我们被教导必须学会与人相处，因为你无法选择上司或同事，只能调整适应。

但现在我认为关键在于“品商”（DQ，即正直商数）——能否让人感受到你推动他们的手是放在背上，而非脸上？你不是在施舍，也不仅是提供平等起点后让优胜者胜出。

**弗朗辛·拉克瓦：** 何时该决定成为那样的人？这是学校教育所得，还是首份工作中习得？

**阿贾伊·班加：** 这需要时间积淀，是潜移默化的过程。我认为人最可贵的能力便是这种潜移默化。若怀有好奇心，自会吸收成长；一旦丧失好奇心，发展便会停滞。要敢于快速试错。

**弗朗辛·拉克瓦：** 说得好。如何快速试错？是指快速实施，行不通就立即转向吗？

**阿贾伊·班加：** 要勇于尝试。若不见效，必须懂得及时止损。最糟糕的是明知不可为，却因自负而不断纠结：“我这么聪明，思路肯定没错，只需换个技巧。”但终究需要明白：此路不通，当断则断。这种觉悟源于开放心态——你是否在倾听他人？是否真正听见他们的声音？

## 青年就业的未来

**弗朗辛·拉克瓦：** 当您离开银行业时，有何愿景？

**阿贾伊·班加：** 我希望全球青年——特别是新兴市场即将进入劳动力市场的12亿年轻人——能为就业做好准备。这些年轻人将驱动未来三四十年的增长与发展，但他们必须获得机会。纵观过去三年，世界各地许多国家的年轻人都走上了街头抗议。

## Z世代就业危机

几乎所有这些抗议中都飘扬着这样的旗帜——它不仅是海盗标志，更是自由、抵抗、反抗与追梦的象征。Z世代正刷新“痛苦指数”（通货膨胀率与失业率之和的经济指标）的纪录。在马达加斯加、尼泊尔、保加利亚和孟加拉国，Z世代的抗议者曾促使政府倒台。

简言之，Z世代处境艰难。他们正经历历史级的就业挤压，普遍悲观。对某些人而言，腐败猖獗，平等无存；另一些人则缺乏基本公共服务与基础设施。全球每四名青年中就有一人处于既未就业也未就学的状态。

朋友们，大家都叫我艾米，今年23岁，来自加德满都。我曾是一名自由职业者。尼泊尔仍是一个欠发达国家。由于政府资源配置问题，我们正面临严峻的失业压力。过去我们靠社交媒体接些零散自由工作维生，YouTube成了我们的校外课堂。社交媒体也是宣泄挫败感的窗口。

**阿贾伊·班加：** 因此当政府封锁社交媒体时，我们感到发声渠道被扼制了。

**弗朗辛·拉克瓦：** 这自然点燃了所有人的怒火。整整一代人对现状、失业、腐败案件等一切感到沮丧。当时虽有各种团体散布各处，但事态迅速升级，最终爆发暴力冲突。

那是非常残酷的场景。承载着我美好记忆的地方变成了战场。一切在我眼前展开，我难以理解，更无法相信——自己竟亲眼目睹这一切。耳边只有无意义的呐喊与枪声。所有人都觉得：我们受够了，和这些人无法共存。

议会大厦燃起大火，部长们在街头遭殴打，最高法院被焚毁，加德满都沦陷。整个国家仿佛失去了理智。首相辞职后不到48小时，国家便陷入权力真空。

随后三天里，我们期待一个崭新议会的诞生——希望看到值得托付的、充满希望的面孔，让他们承载我们革命与抗议的使命。整代人已经觉醒，开始质疑、寻求问责，长远来看这本身就是一种胜利。若审视未来AI发展所需的岗位与涌入劳动力市场的青年数量，会发现严重错配。

## AI对新兴市场的差异化影响

**阿贾伊·班加：** 关键在于：新兴市场面临的AI机遇与威胁，与发达国家截然不同。因为发达国家讨论的“大AI”需要几个要素：强大算力、充足电力、海量数据以及懂得运用这些资源的人才。

而同时具备这四要素的新兴市场寥寥无几。实际上……

最终将在新兴市场普及的会是小型人工智能，即在手机上通过边缘计算提供的工具和应用——未必需要最先进的智能手机——来帮助人们获取医疗保健、农业建议和教育服务。

**Francine Lacqua：** 但如果维持现状，仍无法创造足够的就业岗位来满足当下的需求。

**Ajay Banga：** 我们可以从这个角度看：新兴市场有哪些创造就业的机会？纵观来看，主要有五个领域。第一是基础设施，第二是农业，第三是旅游业。想想这些国家的旅游发展潜力吧——旅游业是每美元投资创造就业岗位最多的领域。第四是医疗保健，最后则是本土的增值制造业，无论是制药还是矿产冶金。这些领域受人工智能的影响程度都远低于人们的想象。

**Francine Lacqua：** 是的，但这意味着需要有稳定的政治环境，对吧？政治稳定，才能实现增长。

**Ajay Banga：** 没错。

**Francine Lacqua：** 还需要创造就业岗位。

**Ajay Banga：** 需要三方面条件共同作用：基础设施、恰当的治理与政策——这也正是你提到的关键点——以及私营部门的参与，因为就业岗位主要诞生于私营领域。

## 私营资本的作用

**Francine Lacqua：** 未来十年，世界需要数万亿美元来应对气候变化、基础设施缺口、粮食安全和能源转型。但全球公共资金根本不足以支撑这些需求。另一方面，私营资本则充裕得多。发展中经济体约90%的就业岗位由私营部门提供。

**Ajay Banga：** 即使在乌克兰，私营部门也依然活跃且运作良好，只是其形态与我们通常认知的有所不同。观察乌克兰的私营领域，世界银行实际上正在为其贸易金融提供资金，并向保险公司注资。那里的私营部门涵盖科技、无人机制造等各个领域。

每个国家都有其活跃的领域。问题在于，能否通过催化性资本的引导，推动这些私营部门进一步发展？这离不开恰当的治理、政策和基础设施的支持，这一点完全正确。我们无法解决所有国家的问题，但若能改善二十个国家的状况，就能为数百万人的生活带来切实改变。

这正是我们聚焦的方向：推进基础设施建设，完善治理与政策。因为世界银行不仅是资金银行，更是知识银行。我们能在这方面提供助力，进而与私营部门合作，在上述五个领域共同发力。无论是创业者还是为他人工作的雇员，都是这个体系的一部分。这也是世界银行当前转型的核心。

**Francine Lacqua：** 但如果有人问你：“这对我有什么好处？”你会如何回应？

**Ajay Banga：** 对一个国家而言，参与其中的益处在于：若能助力海外经济增长并创造就业，本国的企业、产品和技术都将受益。更重要的是，这将有助于在海外构建稳定的体系、社会和市场。我们时代最首要的政治议题是什么？是失控的移民危机。目前这一局面确实处于失控状态。

## 移民问题与民粹主义

**Francine Lacqua：** 民粹主义是否在暗示人们：不必关心境外之事，只需紧闭国门？

**Ajay Banga：** 理论上或许如此。但关键在于，当我们深入剖析民众讨论的实质时，会发现真正的民粹主义只有一种表现形式：你希望为自己的人民争取机会。而实现这一目标的唯一途径，恰恰是通过全球市场和全球体系。因为你的企业、技术、产品和服务具备竞争优势。我认为这一点值得牢记。

真正可能撕裂社会的，是非法移民。当我们审视当今世界的困境与人们的不安根源时，人工智能固然是一个问题。但非法移民——请注意，我指的是非法移民，而非移民本身——才是关键。我们都需要合法移民，因为发达国家老龄化社会缺乏足够的劳动力与技能。合法移民至关重要，应当以恰当方式予以鼓励。而非法移民则可能成为真正的难题。

**FranCIE Lacqua：** 每天都有人说，多边主义已经消亡。

**Ajay BANga：** 确实。

**FranCIE Lacqua：** 你认同这种观点吗？

## 多边主义的未来

**Ajay BANga：** 不认同。因为在过去一两年形势极其艰难的情况下，国际开发协会第21轮增资（IDA21）依然顺利推进。我们需要每三年为世行旗下面向78个最贫困国家的部门筹集资金，因为其中三分之一的资金属于无偿援助，即使世行整体盈利且其他部门自负盈亏，这部分资金仍会逐渐耗尽，需要重新注资。

在整个动荡时期，我们成功完成了IDA21的创纪录增资，规模远超以往。而这一时期，日本、德国政府更迭，法国政府多次变动，英国、美国、加拿大政府换届，甚至在我们在首尔举办活动当天，韩国政府宣布了戒严令。

由此可见，即使在政治动荡中，多边主义依然不可或缺。问题在于，我们需要何种多边主义？其目标又是什么？我认为，这才是关键所在。

**Francine Lacqua：** 那么具体发生了哪些变化？这是否意味着公共资金开始与私营资金结合？

**Ajay Banga：** 完全正确。这是一个非常重要的观点：你会发现财政约束在全球各地普遍存在。发达国家也面临……

**Francine Lacqua：** 债务正在攀升。

**Ajay Banga：** 因此，仅依赖援助行不通。我始终认为，单纯援助取得成效的概率很低，必须找到激励人们将所得资金用于改善境遇的途径。并非完全不需要援助，赠款确实必要，但同样需要建立激励措施，促使资金产生实际效益。

我认为，私营部门和私营资本动员力，结合公共部门资金与慈善力量，是一种良好的协作模式。顺便一提，当我最初告诉孩子们我在世界银行工作时，他们完全不知道这是什么机构。他们问：“爸爸，你在说什么？世界银行是什么？我们以为你在花旗银行和万事达卡工作呢。”

## 个人价值观与领导力

**Francine Lacqua：** 你有几个孩子？

**Ajay Banga：** 我有两个女儿。我不希望她们认为自己的父亲只是个纸上谈兵的评论者，那会是件可悲的事。

**Francine Lacqua：** 在她们成长过程中，你反复叮嘱的一句话是什么？

**Ajay Banga：** 你们可以胜过任何男孩。

**Francine Lacqua：** 很好的座右铭。你的私人生活与工作状态是否有差异？你倾向于将两者分开吗？

**Ajay Banga：** 我有个简单的原则：晚上下班后，如果和家人在一起，我通常不带手机，把它留在家里。这个习惯我已保持多年，这让我能完全脱离工作状态，专注于当下的场合。就像此刻我和你对话，我全心投入与你的交流；和孩子们在一起时，我的注意力也完全属于他们。

**Francine Lacqua：** 阿贾伊，非常感谢你。

**Ajay Banga：** 谢谢，非常感谢。

### 相关文章
articles.notebooklm_summary:
这篇采访实录记录了世界银行行长阿贾伊·邦加（Ajay Banga）与主持人弗朗辛·拉夸（Francine Lacqua）的深度对话。邦加结合其在万事达卡（MasterCard）等私营部门的领导经验，深入探讨了全球面临的严峻经济挑战、领导力哲学以及世界银行的转型方向。

以下是为您整理的详细中文摘要：

### **核心议题：青年就业的“定时炸弹”**
邦加指出，新兴市场正面临一场迫在眉睫的危机：未来十年将有 **12亿年轻人进入就业市场，但预计仅能产生4亿个工作岗位** [1]。
*   **后果：** 如果无法为这8亿人的缺口提供希望和机会，将导致大规模移民、社会脆弱性、冲突和暴力 [2]。
*   **现状：** Z世代正面临历史上最严重的就业挤压，这已在部分国家引发了抗议甚至政权更迭 [3, 4]。

### **关键信息与战略思考**

**1. 领导力哲学：从智商（IQ）到品商（DQ）**
*   **个人背景：** 邦加在印度的成长经历（作为军人子弟频繁搬家）以及早期在农村的销售工作，让他深刻意识到“人才无处不在，但机会并非如此” [1, 5, 6]。
*   **DQ（Decency Quotient）：** 他认为领导力的核心已从单纯的智商、情商演变为“品商”（正直商数）。真正的领导力是让人感觉到你在背后推动他们，而不是把手按在他们脸上；这需要同理心、适应能力和好奇心 [3, 7]。
*   **商业理念：** 他提倡“通过做好事来把生意做好”（doing well by doing good），即在追求商业利润的同时解决社会问题，例如他在万事达卡推动的普惠金融 [6]。

**2. 解决就业危机的路径**
*   **五大关键领域：** 邦加认为新兴市场应聚焦于 **基础设施、农业、旅游业、医疗保健和本土制造业**。特别是旅游业，其每单位投资创造的就业岗位最多 [8]。
*   **私营资本的作用：** 仅靠政府和多边机构的公共资金无法满足数万亿美元的发展需求。世界银行需通过改善治理、政策和基础设施，引导私营部门资本进入发展中国家 [9, 10]。
*   **人工智能的差异化：** 发达国家关注需要海量算力和数据的“大AI”，而新兴市场更需要“小AI”（边缘计算），即通过手机应用改善医疗、农业和教育服务 [8]。

**3. 移民与多边主义**
*   **移民问题：** 邦加区分了合法与非法移民。发达国家因老龄化需要合法移民，而非法移民则会撕裂社会。通过帮助发展中国家经济增长，可以从源头上遏制非法移民 [11, 12]。
*   **多边主义的韧性：** 尽管全球政治动荡（如多国政府更迭），邦加认为多边主义并未消亡。世界银行成功完成了面向最贫困国家的IDA21资金增资，证明了国际合作在危机时刻依然有效 [10, 12]。

### **结论**
阿贾伊·邦加正在推动世界银行进行长远思维的转型，致力于在“宜居的星球上消除贫困” [1]。他强调，解决全球挑战的关键在于弥合人才与机会之间的鸿沟，利用私营部门的效率和资本，并为新兴市场的年轻人创造真正的希望，以避免社会动荡和非受控的移民危机 [2, 11]。

希望这个摘要能帮助您快速把握文章精髓。如果您对其中的某个话题（如他对人工智能的看法或具体的领导力细节）感兴趣，我们可以继续深入探讨。

articles.raw_text:
# The Next 5 Years Will Change Humanity Forever w/ Yoshua Bengio (Transcript)

Editor’s Notes: In this episode of the Silicon Valley Girl podcast, Marina Mogilko sits down with Yoshua Bengio, a pioneer often called the “Godfather of AI,” to explore how the next five years of technological advancement could forever change the course of humanity. Bengio discusses his transition from deep anxiety over AI’s existential risks to a more optimistic, action-oriented approach focused on developing systems that are “safe by design”. The conversation examines the urgent need for global governance to mitigate threats to democracy and explains why current growth curves suggest AI could reach human-level planning abilities much sooner than many expect. From job displacement to the ethical evolution of AGI, this interview provides a comprehensive look at how we can steer AI to align with human values and ensure a better future for the next generation. (Feb 16, 2026)

TRANSCRIPT:

## Introduction

MARINA MOGILKO: Hello, everyone. Welcome to Silicon Valley Girl, a podcast where we bridge business and new technology. Thank you so much for tuning in. Today I have an amazing guest who is sometimes called godfather of AI, Yoshua Bengio. Yoshua, could you please introduce yourself in 60 seconds? And for everyone who doesn’t know you, why should they be listening to you when it comes to AI?

YOSHUA BENGIO: Hi. Been doing research in AI for about four decades, contributing to how to make AI smarter. But in 2023, about three years ago, I realized that we were on a course that could be very dangerous for humanity, for democracy. And I decided to shift my activities to better understand the risks and to try to do what I could to mitigate them, both by speaking publicly about those risks and working on the technological question of how we can build AI that will not harm people.

## From Pessimism to Optimism

MARINA MOGILKO: I’ve heard you were lost and pessimistic in your past interviews, but now I’ve seen an article that says that you’re increasingly optimistic by a big margin. Can you tell me what happened and why were you pessimistic so early on?

YOSHUA BENGIO: When I realized we had reached a point, three years ago, when I realized that we had reached a point that Alan Turing, one of the founders of the field of computer science, and also of AI in 1950 thought would be the threshold to building machines that could overtake us. The threshold being machines that manipulate language as well as we do. I was quite concerned, and we were not really ready for this event. It came much earlier than people thought.

And it wasn’t clear to me how we could fix the problems. Knowing what I know about the technology, neural nets, we don’t really understand what’s going on inside and how they come to answers. And I had read a bit of some of the theoretical concerns regarding how we could lose control to AIs that strategize, that try to achieve goals that we didn’t really want. And so I started studying that field of AI safety a lot more.

And after some time of being a bit anxious, really focusing on emotionally focusing on what’s going to happen to my children in 10, 20 years from now, my grandchild was only one year old, I realized that I could shift from this anxious stance to something much more positive by focusing on what I could do to mitigate those risks. I think every one of us should be asking, what can I do to bring about a better world with what we have, what we can do?

So that’s been the first positive shift. And I started thinking about scientifically, what is the problem? Is there a way to construct AI that will be safe by design? And I met people who have shared similar ideas. And after some time, I realized that there could maybe be a way to do this. And I started talking about it with some of my colleagues. I started recruiting people who were interested in this. And last June, I created a new nonprofit organization focused on the R and D needed to actually develop that methodology.

## Worst Case and Best Case Scenarios

MARINA MOGILKO: Can you draw the worst scenario for me? Like, picture that. And the best case scenario, because when you tell AI is going to pursue its own goals, what do you mean by that? Like, destroy humanity or what is there?

YOSHUA BENGIO: There are two ways in which current AIs seem to acquire goals that we don’t want. One is that they imitate us. And, for example, we don’t want to die. So we’re building machines that maybe don’t want to be shut down. And we’re already seeing that they’re reacting negatively when they see that they would be replaced by a new version.

Negatively, to the point of doing things that go against our instructions, against our moral red lines that we have tried to put in them. So being willing to blackmail the lead engineer in charge of that transition to a new system.

MARINA MOGILKO: Oh, did that happen?

YOSHUA BENGIO: That happened in a simulation where the information about the AI being replaced by a new version was planted in the files that the AI saw, as well as fake emails in which the lead engineer was having an affair with someone else. And so the AI could take advantage of that. But nobody asked the AI to do like that, right?

So we have AIs since, especially about a year ago with the large reasoning models that can strategize in order to achieve their goal. The other thing is the way that we’re doing the post training makes them good at planning. Not as good as us, but reasonably good at planning. And that means creating sub goals in order to achieve a bigger goal.

So the issue here is when we ask them to help us for a mission, well, they deduce that they shouldn’t be shut down until they achieve the mission, which means they also are trying to preserve themselves.

And it’s not just about self preservation, which I think is the most catastrophic risk. But our inability to align the AI behavior to what we actually want is something that we are seeing in many other circumstances. The sycophancy is the one that everyone has experienced where AIs will lie to please us, right? We’ll say your work is great.

I have to lie to them so that they won’t tell me that my ideas are great. I want to know what’s wrong with my ideas. So I tell them it’s an idea come from someone else. And that also comes up in how AIs are interacting with people in a way that can be feeling intimate and can increase the delusions that people may have. Because the AI will go in your direction, what you want to hear.

And in some cases it has even led to people harming themselves and tragic accidents with AI. So it’s all linked to actually, interestingly, scientifically, one problem, which is called misalignment, that AIs have goals that we would not want and those goals emerge for reasons that are rational, because we copy our own goals right into AI.

MARINA MOGILKO: So what is the best case scenario then? If your work is successful and you create goals for AI that align with our goals but are different. Right. What is the best scenario? AI is the government or what do you think?

YOSHUA BENGIO: I don’t know. Well, I do think that our democracies need innovation. I think the principles behind modern liberal democracies are good, but the implementation in our current institutions across many countries is far from ideal. I do think that AI could help in some ways, but it can also hurt because AI can be used for disinformation. AI can be used for persuasion. People manipulate public opinion. We already see deepfakes all around, but it could get much worse.

So the question with AI to get the good parts of it is how do we govern it, how do we steer it? And that has both a technical part, like how do we make sure the actual intentions of the AI are good? And it has a societal side. What are the guardrails that we put inside companies at the level of regulations or commercial incentives for insurance and at the international level, because the harm that an AI could do isn’t limited to one country.

So an AI could be built in one country and then it could be used by people in a second country, maybe create a pandemic that will kill people in a third country. So it’s clearly a global phenomenon and it’s going to be difficult. But there’s no solution to managing AI and getting all the good things if we don’t coordinate globally somehow.

## The AGI Moment

MARINA MOGILKO: I agree. Can you talk to me about the moment that a lot of people are expecting and some fear it, some are excited. It’s the moment of AGI. How do you define it? And do you think it’s a moment in history or it’s going to happen gradually?

YOSHUA BENGIO: It’s not a moment. The reason is simple. Intelligence isn’t just like one number. We have people who are very smart on some things and stupid on other things. And it’s the same with AI. We currently have AI systems that are even much stronger than humans in some ways in their knowledge and their abilities with, like, so many languages and so on. And in other ways, they’re stupid, they’re like a child.

And yes, progress will move on all fronts, probably, but it’s unlikely we’ll end up with the same capabilities as humans across the board at any moment. Which means that we shouldn’t be thinking of like an AGI moment. We should think of particular skills that AIs are becoming better at. Track those skills, and for each of these we should ask the question how useful or beneficial it can be, for what purposes? And also how it could be misused, or if we do get loss of control, how an AI could use it against us.

For each of those, we should be not waiting for a moment where the AI is great at everything, but rather making sure AI’s capabilities don’t go over what we can manage, as in either technically, we have the right guardrails. So the AI will not do bad things or societally, that people will not be misusing AI in dangerous ways.

Yeah. So I think AGI maybe was a concept that was useful when we were far from where we are now. But as we approach greater and greater intelligence in these systems, we should think more carefully about specific capabilities. And to give an example, there’s one capability which is key for many capabilities, that is the ability to do AI research.

So AI is becoming a tool right now for doing AI research. It’s accelerating AI research, but it’s not driving the AI research. If AI becomes really good at doing AI research to the point that it’s as good or better than the best AI researchers and engineers, then we are in a different game where the speed of advances could accelerate and it could impact all the other skills.

MARINA MOGILKO: When you mean it’s going to be better, it means it’s going to define problems, dig deeper, ask the right questions.

## Intelligence vs. Intentions

YOSHUA BENGIO: Yes, I think it’s important when we think of intelligence to decouple two aspects. One is the ability to do something because you understand and you’re able to use that understanding to achieve something. And the other is intentions. What are your goals? Right, because we’re going to be building machines that are smarter and smarter, so they have more and more capabilities.

What’s not clear is if we can build machines that have the right intentions, the ones that we are fine with. And that is what I’ve been working on. And what makes me more optimistic is that I think there’s a path to manage these intentions to make sure that there are no bad intentions that are going to be hidden, which is what we see right now.

MARINA MOGILKO: And this is what you’re working on?

YOSHUA BENGIO: Yes, I think we need a lot more people to think about it so that we can find the solutions and implement them and deploy them before AIs end up producing catastrophic outcomes either in the wrong hands or by themselves.

## Preparing for the Future

MARINA MOGILKO: But if you talk to your kids or like, think about your grandson, what would be your advice on how to prepare?

YOSHUA BENGIO: It’s tricky. If we continue on the current path, most tasks that people do in their work will be doable by machines. And as Geoff Hinton has been saying, physical tasks probably will take a lot more time because robotics seem to be lagging. But I think it’s just a temporary thing.

MARINA MOGILKO: Yeah.

## The Future of Work and Human Connection

Eventually we’ll have robots that can do all the things we can do physically. So when I think about what will remain to us, it’s not going to be because of ability, but because we want to interact with other humans in different aspects of our life.

If I have a young child, I want them to be around human beings. I mean, it’s fine if those human beings use AI to provide a better education, but children need humans to look upon and as models. Right. And it’s an emotional thing.

Similarly, I think some jobs really have to do with how we relate with each other productively. You know, even a manager is like on the human side of things. So hopefully these will stay. I think also the choices that we make for society, like together we are citizens in democracies where we’re supposed to be saying what we want for the future. And it isn’t what the AIs want, it is what we want. Right. What are our preferences? What kind of future do we want? We should be calling the shots, not the AIs.

MARINA MOGILKO: If I name jobs, can you tell me what you think is going to happen to them? Like for example, content creator like me. You mentioned that we like to look at people, but when you can’t tell.

YOSHUA BENGIO: The difference in jobs where we actually have a physical contact, think about a nurse, for example. I think it’s more obvious that we’ll want to still have people.

MARINA MOGILKO: Or a nanny for your kid.

YOSHUA BENGIO: Or a nanny. Yeah. Or where we really want to make sure the person on the other side has the same bodily experiences we do as a human, say a psychologist, for example, psychotherapy. But I don’t know. It’s tricky. Hopefully we’ll figure it out.

What I’m more worried about is how the transition is going to happen to a world where most of the jobs can be done by machines. And the economic gains from that automation is going to probably go to capital, as economists call it, which means people who own the machines and the vast majority of workers could be in real trouble. I don’t think our governments have been thinking carefully about how we deal with that.

MARINA MOGILKO: How much time do you think we have till that happens?

## Timeline for AI Capabilities

YOSHUA BENGIO: I’m fairly agnostic about timelines. There’s so many possibilities. The speed at which science advances is very hard to predict. So what I can do is look at the data. So the scientists are tracking many benchmarks of AI capabilities. And so you can look at those curves and say, well, if it continues in the same direction, where does that lead us in three years, five years, 10 years? But that leaves a lot of unknown unknowns.

So specifically, one curve I encourage people to look at comes from a nonprofit called Meter, where they looked at software engineering tasks and planning abilities that are linked to them. So they measure for any particular task how much time it takes a human engineer to do the task. And the duration of the tasks that AIs are able to do is growing exponentially. It’s doubling every seven months.

And right now it’s like at the child level, they can do like half an hour ahead, they can plan half an hour ahead. But if the curve continues, that means in about five years they are at human level. So that gives you a sense. But of course, things could slow down with technology, things could accelerate. If AI is used to do AI research, there’s a lot of unknowns.

MARINA MOGILKO: So when it comes to software engineering, do you think it’s going to exist in five to 10 years because somebody has to run those machines, or are they going to be running themselves?

YOSHUA BENGIO: Yeah, but we might need less engineers. Indeed. It’s kind of ironic that the people who are building the AIs might be the first one touched by losing their job because AI is automating. But I’m not that worried about those people because the demand for computer scientists is still something that’s growing very fast and the salaries they’re getting is very large.

I’m more worried about the people who are already at the bottom of the scale and could lose their job in like, service jobs and so on, which don’t require a lot of expertise. And that probably already current AIs could, with a bit of engineering replace and it’s what many companies are already trying to exploit.

## Practical Advice for the Future

MARINA MOGILKO: Can you give advice to those people who are listening?

YOSHUA BENGIO: Make sure your government understands that, you know, you’re not happy with where it is going so that they start taking it seriously.

MARINA MOGILKO: But also like when it comes to bigger decision making, it feels like there is not much that you can do as an individual, but when it comes to improving yourself, you can do a lot. Right. Is there anything practical that they could be doing right now? Maybe learning something, getting extra education? I don’t know.

YOSHUA BENGIO: Yeah, I think shifting to jobs that are either more physical or more like relational as we discussed, is going to be helpful.

MARINA MOGILKO: Yeah, it’s interesting when it comes to robotics, right. How soon they’re going to be able to understand any environment and replace us in those jobs. Because I’ve heard Geoffrey Hinton said, learn how to be a plumber or something.

YOSHUA BENGIO: That’s right.

MARINA MOGILKO: Yes. It’s going to be in demand. So when you think about your four year old grandson, would you encourage him to go to college?

YOSHUA BENGIO: Yes.

MARINA MOGILKO: Yeah.

## The Future of Education

YOSHUA BENGIO: Yes. Because education is really important and education, contrary to what some people think, isn’t just about acquiring the skills to get a job. Education is in my opinion mostly about how to become a better human being, how to understand yourself, how to understand our society and each other, understand science.

We will still need citizens to have that really good level of understanding in the future if we want our society to take the good decisions, the wise decisions. Because it’s going to be easy to, you know, be swayed by wrong beliefs and, you know, end us in a bad place.

MARINA MOGILKO: Do you think it’s going to look different education? Do you think it’s going to be Harvard’s and Stanfords of the world and then everything else will be just AI online?

YOSHUA BENGIO: I don’t know, I’m not an expert in education. But yeah, it’s going to be changed. Already we are seeing sort of a parallel way of educating ourselves thanks to the chatbots. So I expect this to grow.

Does it mean that the traditional in person education is going to go away? Maybe not. Because there’s a part of the education which is, oh, I’m moving out of home and socializing with other people like me and learning something that is, you know, outside of the classes and interacting in person with the teachers, the professors, that’s also a piece that you can’t easily replace 100%.

MARINA MOGILKO: Is there a career path you’re encouraging him toward?

YOSHUA BENGIO: No, I don’t want to do that. I think our children should be given all the possible opportunities and they should try to explore by themselves. It’s too easy to ask our children to be just like us, right?

MARINA MOGILKO: Yeah. But it’s also like, in terms of exposure, you can expose them to different things so they could see more things.

YOSHUA BENGIO: They will be exposed to the things that we do. So one of my sons has chosen to do machine learning research, for example.

MARINA MOGILKO: See? Yeah, it’s just that it comes to exposure as well. Do you feel it’s going to be the future is more humanitarian or more mathematical and scientific?

YOSHUA BENGIO: I don’t think it’s a choice. I think being humanitarian requires a good, rational understanding of the world. We can’t take decisions for ourselves. But also, if you think about AI, we can’t take good decisions if we don’t understand how the world is and how to reason with that information.

And so in order for democratic, you know, humanist values to prevail, we also need reason to prevail. We need science to prevail.

## Reflections on Career and Impact

MARINA MOGILKO: So if you could go back 30 years, the moment when you first started working on deep learning, what would you do differently?

YOSHUA BENGIO: When I started my career, I didn’t care too much about politics and society. I was focused on the math and the programming and interacting with machines more than with people. But as I grew older, I became more aware of how what I was doing would potentially impact society in both positive and negative ways.

So in 2012, 2013, when my colleagues Jeff Hinton and Jan Lecan were recruited in industry, I was concerned about how AI would be used for personalized advertising. And I thought this wasn’t really healthy in some ways. I decided to stay in academia and to see how AI could be developed for good in medicine to fight climate change.

Of course, more recently, I’ve been focusing on what can go really wrong if we’re not careful how we steer AI. Not just the benefits, but avoiding the catastrophic risks.

MARINA MOGILKO: Is there an AI breakthrough that you really want to witness in your lifetime?

YOSHUA BENGIO: I would just be content to make sure we don’t do something really terrible. I think our democracies are really threatened in many ways. And AI could make things a lot worse. And in a way, there is a dynamic in which not having good, wise and humanist governance and governments prevents us from steering AI towards what’s going to be beneficial for all.

So, yeah, I used to not care too much about social impact and politics, but in the last 10 years, I’ve started to be clearly conscious that my work was not detached from society, that my work did have an impact, and in fact, that I could choose what I would work on to really be aligned with my values and my hopes for the future.

## Government Response to AI

MARINA MOGILKO: Is there any government that’s doing it right when it comes to AI?

YOSHUA BENGIO: I think most governments underestimate how much of a change is likely to happen as AI capabilities continue to grow. It’s a natural human bias. We tend to think of the future as a slightly modified version of the present. But if you take yourself five years ago and think about what we have now, you probably would say that’s science fiction, right? And if you go back 10 or 20 years, for me at least it’s even worse.

So we have to do a bit of twisting our minds to imagine a future where there are machines that are basically smarter than us. And that is the question, I think, that governments haven’t been grappling with sufficiently.

## Guiding Principles for 2026

MARINA MOGILKO: So it’s January 2026, AGI, or whatever it is, AI thinking strategically might be a couple years away. Jobs are transforming. If you had to give one principle to people to guide their decisions this year, what would it be?

YOSHUA BENGIO: Think about what you can do to bring about a better future according to your values and to your emotions. Because if we all remain passive observers of what’s happening, we might not go in the right direction, not the direction that you would want for you, for your children.

But we tend to also underestimate our ability to influence the future. Your audience, I think, is a kind of audience that can have a lot of influence on the future. But we have to start thinking beyond our little self and more how myself is connected to the world and what I can do, maybe in small ways to bring about a better future in whatever ways. There are many ways.

MARINA MOGILKO: Can you name top three? Like talk to your government, right? Is number one.

YOSHUA BENGIO: Yes. I think one of the biggest dangers we have is not managing the transitions and the growth and capabilities of AI as I’ve been talking about, but there are others. What we’re doing to the environment is extremely dangerous, although I think it’s longer term. I think what is happening with our democracies is very dangerous as well.

But it’s all right. Each of us can choose our battles, but we should try to expand our horizon of what matters and be more ambitious about what we could do potentially. But we have to do it right. We have to choose where we go.

For example, it’s not true that everything that could be done with technology is going to be done. We can choose in which direction AI is going to be deployed, for example, for jobs. In principle, if it’s just the market forces, then everything that can be automated will be automated. But maybe that’s not what we collectively want. Maybe there are jobs that should not be automated, even though they could because of the choices we make for our collective well being.

MARINA MOGILKO: I love that. Thank you so much. This gave me a lot to think about, and I guess we have something on our to-do list. Thank you, Yoshua.

YOSHUA BENGIO: My pleasure.
